title: 0001_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_CHeSRI_showcase_portable-suctioning-device.html
text: Home Portable Medical-Grade Suctioning Device Portable Medical-Grade Suctioning Device Second generation prototype – the use of piston vacuum pump Project Background Airway suctioning devices can be lifesaving during critical situations. However, the current medical-grade suctioning devices are not compact, portable, or battery-operated. This project has developed prototypes of portable airway suctioning device that can meet the following user requirements: High performance capability Compact, portable, and lightweight Battery-operation of approximately two hours Medical homecare equipment grade Ergonomically-friendly and user intuitive The Team Faculty Assoc Prof Meredith Yeung , Health and Social Sciences, Project Supervisor Students Kwa Chee Yong, Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) Mechatronics, Student Team Member, Class of 2016 Tan Li Jun, Bachelor of Engineering (Honours) Mechatronics, Student Team Member, Class of 2017

title: 0002_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_CHeSRI_showcase_rfid-based-reminder-system.html
text: Home RFID-based reminder system to improve infection control compliance RFID-Based Reminder System to Improve Infection Control Compliance WHO’s five moments of hand hygiene Healthcare provider wearing RFID tag, standing in close proximity, activates the RFID reminder indicator and captures the identity of the healthcare provider Hand Sanitizer Holder with 3D printed base and embedded sensor ❮ ❯ Project Background Adherence to the five moments of hand hygiene is required by healthcare professionals while attending to their patients. Poor hand hygiene may lead to patients being more susceptible to developing Healthcare-Associated Infections (HAIs). Currently, hand hygiene adherence is measured through direct observation, which is labour intensive, expensive to administer, and unreliable. The SIT team aims to use engineering technology to improve the accuracy of the monitoring process, to encourage good hand hygiene habits. An RFID-based reminder system prototype allows documenting of The SIT team aims to use engineering technology to improve the accuracy of the monitoring process, to encourage good hand hygiene habits. An RFID-based reminder system prototype allows documenting of hand hygiene practices, and is being tested for reliability and robustness. The Team Faculty Elaine Siow, Health and Social Sciences, Principal Investigator Assoc Prof Alfred Tan , Engineering, Co-Principal Investigator Students Billy Chew, BEng Mechatronics, University of Glasgow Agnes Ho, BSc in Nursing Practice, University of Manchester

title: 0003_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_CHeSRI_showcase_video-game-repository-parents.html
text: Home Video Game Repository for Parents Video Game Repository for Parents Final Report Presentation at Kingmaker Consultancy Pte Ltd. Student Assistants presenting the VGRP Prototype Student Assistants presenting their initial Literature Reviews to the Industry Partner ❮ ❯ Project Background A study conducted by Choo et. al. (2010) showed that approximately 9% of video gamers in Singapore, the 5th highest in the world, demonstrate pathological symptoms.. Parents are presented with a significant challenge in managing their children’s video gaming habits. There are few resources available for parents to learn more about managing their children's gaming habits. Existing game information sites typically adopt a content-centric approach, which does not alleviate parental concerns, such as time displacement and social interactions. This project seeks to develop a Video Games Repository for Parents (VGRP) prototype, which serves as a video game information resource. It endeavours to educate displacement and social interactions. This project seeks to develop a Video Games Repository for Parents (VGRP) prototype, which serves as a video game information resource. It endeavours to educate parents on the various issues related to children’s video gaming usage, as well as to provide parents with relevant information about the video games their children are consuming. Such information is essential for parental management of children’s video game consumption. Finally, the resource can provide youth video gamers with the ability and information needed to make informed decisions about their own video gaming behaviours. The Team Faculty Assoc Prof Jiow Hee Jhee, Health and Social Sciences, Principal Investigator Kenneth Woo, Business, Communication and Design, Professional Officer Students Muhammad Helmi Bin Elias, Health and Social Sciences Chew Ling Ling, Health and Social Sciences Ng Xin Yu, Health and Social Sciences Nina Lim Jia Hui, Health and Social Sciences Rayvinder Jit Muhammad Helmi Bin Elias, Health and Social Sciences Chew Ling Ling, Health and Social Sciences Ng Xin Yu, Health and Social Sciences Nina Lim Jia Hui, Health and Social Sciences Rayvinder Jit Sing Athwal, Health and Social Sciences Adil Zhuri Bin Abdul Hamid, Health and Social Sciences Julianah Binte Mohamed Jumadi, Health and Social Sciences Tan Yun Ting Jasmine, Health and Social Sciences Muhamed Mubarak Bin Mohamed Johari, Health and Social Sciences Poon Mu Qian Timothy, Health and Social Sciences Darrick Chew Wei Le, Health and Social Sciences Cindy Peh Wan Xin, Health and Social Sciences Suhaila Binte Zainal Shah, Health and Social Sciences Jill See, Business, Communication and Design

As a member of the Design Factory Global Network, DF@SIT will collaborate with a network of innovation hubs in universities and research organisations across five continents in the world, across cultures, time zones and organisational boundaries to foster radical innovations. DF@SIT seeks to help students, academic staff and companies employ design-led innovation tools to pilot ideas and build roadmaps for the changing marketplace. text: Home Design Factory@SIT Design Factory Team Design Factory Team Jeffrey Koh, PhD Associate Professor, Business, Communication and Design Head, Design Factory Jeffrey is an Associate Professor at SIT. In the past he has worked as a fine artist, exhibiting in venues such as the Barbican, Southbank Centre, Singapore Design Festival, Archifest and the Art House Singapore, and was previously an Assistant Director at the National Gallery Singapore. As an experience designer, he has developed catwalks and fashion shows for PRADA and Miu Miu for the Office for Metropolitan Architecture, and helped open the London office of KesselsKramer. He has also worked in the past as a tech entrepreneur and advisor in the areas of artificial intelligence, fine art provenance and crowdsourcing, and is a Fellow of the EF Singapore. He has been featured on Channel News Asia, Digital News Asia, Tech in Asia and the Straits Times. He is a Founder and Advisor at Orobo.io, which is a circular economy on the of the EF Singapore. He has been featured on Channel News Asia, Digital News Asia, Tech in Asia and the Straits Times. He is a Founder and Advisor at Orobo.io, which is a circular economy on the blockchain startup and recipient of the IMDA Blockchain Challenge grant. With Chemistry, a Singapore design consultancy with a satellite office in Amsterdam, Jeffrey has acted as lead consultant and advisor for transformation projects with clients such as NHB, MAS, IMDA, Design Singapore Council, HP, NEC, Konica Minolta, Aviva Singlife, Estee Lauder, and more, and has been invited as keynote speaker for venues such as TEDx Zurich, Pecha Kucha, General Assembly, and others. He currently sits on the advisory board for the NPE Gallery, was a Peer of the Australia Council for the Arts’ Experimental Arts Jury, and was a Researcher and Adjunct Lecturer for UNSW Art & Design in Sydney, Australia, where he codesigned the Bachelor of Fine Arts in Crossmedia degree programme, as well as the Transmedia Jury, and was a Researcher and Adjunct Lecturer for UNSW Art & Design in Sydney, Australia, where he codesigned the Bachelor of Fine Arts in Crossmedia degree programme, as well as the Transmedia Storytelling MOOC on Coursera, which has had over to 70k students enrolled to date. Ho Hung Yi Centre Manager Design Factory Ho Hung Yi is a Senior Professional Officer at the Singapore Institute of Technology. He is an Industrial Designer by training and possesses more than 13 years of work experience in areas of business ventures and creative technoprenuership. He established his career first at Procter and Gamble (P&G) Singapore and then moved on to co-own start-ups that have successfully launched various products of his own design - co-engineering with his partners, primarily in the electric mobility space. Over the years, he has also been actively involved in education, teaching 3D modelling software and prototyping skillsets as an Adjunct Lecturer at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts mobility space. Over the years, he has also been actively involved in education, teaching 3D modelling software and prototyping skillsets as an Adjunct Lecturer at the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts (NAFA). title: 0007_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_NACE_mentorship-support-grant-enterprise-transformation.html
text: Home Workplace Learning Mentorship Support Grant for Enterprise Transformation Mentorship Support Grant for Enterprise Transformation The Enterprise Transformation Project, supported by SkillsFuture Singapore's Mentorship Support Grant, helps businesses boost skills, productivity, and innovation through mentorship and focused projects. Transform Your Business with Expert Mentorship SIT LEARN invites enterprises to enhance their operational capabilities and workforce skills through the Enterprise Transformation Project with the Mentorship Support Grant (MSG) . This initiative, supported and co-funded by SkillsFuture Singapore, empowers businesses to drive sustainable growth and innovation by integrating expert mentorship with practical training. What is the Mentorship Support Grant? The MSG is designed to co-fund consultancy and mentorship services with project-based implementation-led training in helping enterprises implement solutions that lead to business transformation. Through the is designed to co-fund consultancy and mentorship services with project-based implementation-led training in helping enterprises implement solutions that lead to business transformation. Through the programme, organisations can work closely with industry professionals, university academia and SIT LEARN to develop and execute projects in areas such as: Process optimisation and operational excellence Product and service innovation Energy efficiency initiatives Sustainability initiatives Workforce upskilling Food Technology and Innovation Projects eligible for support should aim to: Improve worker skills; Enhance enterprise productivity; and/or, Job redesign. Funding Matters Eligibility Criteria Support Details Enterprise must fulfil the following criteria: SME* or non-SME Registered and operating in Singapore At least 30% local shareholding *Must meet all of the following criteria to be considered a SME: Registered or incorporated in Singapore Employment size should not exceed 200 operating in Singapore At least 30% local shareholding *Must meet all of the following criteria to be considered a SME: Registered or incorporated in Singapore Employment size should not exceed 200 employees Annual sales turnover should not exceed $100 million Eligible enterprises can receive up to 90% funding support, capped at $30,000 per project (for a duration of 6 months). Funding covers mentorship, consultancy, and training activities integral to project execution: $30,000 cap per project SME to be funded up to 90% and non-SME to be funded up to 70% No more than 3 projects per enterprise for funding support Project to be completed within 6 months NACE@SIT Workplace Learning Our Partners Project Application and Implementation Process chevron--up Project Scoping and Submission (3 – 6 weeks) SIT engages enterprises to understand current challenges and identify project scope and intended outcomes. SIT proposes solutions to address the enterprise’s needs. Enterprise decides whether weeks) SIT engages enterprises to understand current challenges and identify project scope and intended outcomes. SIT proposes solutions to address the enterprise’s needs. Enterprise decides whether to apply for MSG and declare SME status and whether it has received other government funding and/or SkillsFuture's MSG funding for more than three times. Upon preliminary agreement on the project scope, deliverables and fees, SIT will submit the project proposal with enterprise details and proposed outcomes to SkillsFuture Singapore for funding approval. chevron--up Review and Approval (5 – 8 weeks) SkillsFuture Singapore will review the proposal for approval and the enterprise may be required to furnish additional details for clarifications when requested. Upon SkillsFuture Singapore's approval, SIT will issue a quotation and agreement for the enterprise’s acceptance. After acceptance, SIT will invoice the enterprise with the nett fees (after SkillsFuture funding and inclusive of approval, SIT will issue a quotation and agreement for the enterprise’s acceptance. After acceptance, SIT will invoice the enterprise with the nett fees (after SkillsFuture funding and inclusive of prevailing GST) of the project. chevron--up Project Commencement and Management (to be completed within 6 months) SIT and enterprise form a project team to commence the project. Progress will be tracked on the attainment of the projects’ KPI milestones. chevron--up Project Completion (within 4 weeks after project completion) Enterprise will submit the project completion sign-off, expenditure statements and relevant invoices/receipts (if any). SIT will administer the post-project survey. Why Choose Us? At SIT LEARN , we blend academic expertise with practical insights to provide impactful mentorship and training. title: 0011_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about.html
text: Home About SIT About As the university for industry and Singapore's first university of applied learning, the Singapore Institute of Technology (SIT) offers industry-relevant degree programmes that prepare its graduates to be work- and future-ready professionals. Its mission is to maximise the potential of its learners and to innovate with industry, through an integrated applied learning and research approach, so as to contribute to the economy and society. The University’s unique pedagogy integrates work and study, embracing authentic learning in a real-world environment through collaborations with key strategic partners. Its focus on applied research with business impact is aimed at helping industry innovate and grow. SIT’s new centralised campus within the larger Punggol Digital District features a vibrant learning environment where academia and industry are tightly integrated with the community. For more information, visit www.SingaporeTech.edu.sg Remote video URL Discover More features a vibrant learning environment where academia and industry are tightly integrated with the community. For more information, visit www.SingaporeTech.edu.sg Remote video URL Discover More About SIT Our History Singapore Institute of Technology was started in 2009 to provide more opportunities for polytechnic graduates to further their education. title: 0017_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_leadership_dr-kwong-yuk-wah.html
text: Home About SIT Leadership Senior Management Dr Kwong Yuk Wah Dr Kwong Yuk Wah Vice President (Digital Transformation) & Chief Digital Officer, Singapore Institute of Technology Dr Kwong Yuk Wah is Vice President (Digital Transformation) and Chief Digital Officer at the Singapore Institute of Technology (SIT). She leads and drives digital transformation and data management at the University. Dr Kwong brings with her extensive experience in conceptualising, implementing and managing large-scale transformation projects involving multiple stakeholders. Her experience spans across the public, private and people sectors, with stints in various agencies and companies such as the National Trades Union Congress (NTUC), Singapore Airlines, Ministry of Finance (MOF), and then Infocomm Development Authority. She also conducts lectures at the National University of Singapore’s School of Computing as an Adjunct Professor. She has played a significant role in major IT initiatives, including Authority. She also conducts lectures at the National University of Singapore’s School of Computing as an Adjunct Professor. She has played a significant role in major IT initiatives, including Singapore Government’s Civil Service Computerisation Programme and various e-Government initiatives. Under her leadership, NTUC clinched the National Infocomm Awards (NIA) 2014 for the Most Innovative Use of Infocomm Technology in the private sector, which is the highest accolade for infocomm innovation in Singapore. Her many achievements include being recognised in the inaugural Singapore 100 Women In Tech (2020) list by Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) and the Singapore Computer Society (SCS). She was awarded the ASEAN CIO Award 2015, and was also celebrated in the inaugural CIO50 in ASEAN 2019 list as one of the top 50 senior technology executives driving innovation and influencing rapid change in the region. Dr Kwong is a Fellow Member of SCS and currently sits on the Board of ASEAN 2019 list as one of the top 50 senior technology executives driving innovation and influencing rapid change in the region. Dr Kwong is a Fellow Member of SCS and currently sits on the Board of the Nanyang Academy of Fine Arts, the Infocomm Technology Projects Advisory Panel (Public Sector) of MOF, and the AI Ethics & Governance Evaluation Board of SCS. She received her Doctor of Business Administration degree from the University of Newcastle, Australia in 2008.

title: 0018_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_leadership_mr-bernard-nee.html
text: Home About SIT Leadership Senior Management Mr Bernard Nee Mr Bernard Nee Deputy President (Industry & Community), Singapore Institute of Technology Mr Bernard Nee is Deputy President (Industry & Community), Singapore Institute of Technology (SIT). He assumed the role on 1 April 2023 after being appointed Vice President (Industry & Community) on 1 Jan 2022. Bernard’s group is responsible for industry and future sensing, corporate communications, as well as collaborations with companies and agencies for applied learning and research. Bernard actively engages companies, trade associations, government agencies and various interest groups to advance SIT’s mission and vision. He brings along deep experience garnered from previous leadership roles at the Economic Development Board (EDB), National Research Foundation (NRF) and the Energy Market Authority (EMA). As the Deputy Chief Executive of EMA (2012-2021), he managed over $200 million in energy R&D funding, and forged partnerships with Research Foundation (NRF) and the Energy Market Authority (EMA). As the Deputy Chief Executive of EMA (2012-2021), he managed over $200 million in energy R&D funding, and forged partnerships with PSA Singapore, Sembcorp Industries, Keppel Offshore & Marine, SP Group and Shell to nurture the energy ecosystem. His team launched the Energy Story (a national blueprint enabling Singapore’s energy transformation), introduced regulatory sandboxes, nurtured a competitive industry workforce and pushed Singapore’s solar and energy storage ambitions. His portfolio also included oversight of EMA’s corporate functions. Prior to 2012, he was with EDB for 18 years, where he assumed senior management roles as Executive Director in charge of Natural Resources, Strategic and Corporate Planning, as well as the Chief Information Officer. He seeded a team to incubate high-growth clusters that could contribute $1 billion to Singapore’s GDP within 10 years. In 2005, he was seconded to the NRF as the as the Chief Information Officer. He seeded a team to incubate high-growth clusters that could contribute $1 billion to Singapore’s GDP within 10 years. In 2005, he was seconded to the NRF as the pioneering Director to establish policies, processes, and systems to manage the $5 billion RIE2010 budget. A recipient of the EDB scholarship, Bernard possesses a Masters in Management of Technology from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, and graduated with an MSc and BSc (with University Honours) in Electrical Engineering from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign.

title: 0019_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_leadership_mr-ronnie-tay.html
text: Home About SIT Leadership Senior Management Mr Ronnie Tay Mr Ronnie Tay Deputy President (Administration) & Chief Financial Officer, Singapore Institute of Technology Mr Ronnie Tay is the Deputy President (Administration) and Chief Financial Officer of the Singapore Institute of Technology (SIT). In this role, Mr Tay is responsible for the resourcing and service support needs of SIT, in the functions of finance, human resource, information technology, estates, legal and library. Prior to joining SIT, Mr Tay served 36 years in the Republic of Singapore Navy and the Singapore Administrative Service. He was the Chief Executive Officer of the National Environment Agency from 2013 to 2018, and the Chief Executive Officer of the Infocomm Development Authority of Singapore from 2007 to 2013. During his career in the Navy, he held various command and staff appointments, and was the Chief of Navy from 2003 to 2007. Mr Tay has previously served on the board of a number of organisations, During his career in the Navy, he held various command and staff appointments, and was the Chief of Navy from 2003 to 2007. Mr Tay has previously served on the board of a number of organisations, including the Maritime & Port Authority of Singapore from 2004 to 2007, Media Development Authority of Singapore from 2008 to 2014, National Institute of Education Council from 2009 to 2014, DSO National Laboratories from 2010 to 2018 and Singapore Institute of Management University from 2011 to 2017. Mr Tay was awarded the Singapore Armed Forces Overseas Scholarship in 1982 and graduated from Oxford University in 1985 with a BA (First Class Honours) in Engineering Science. He also obtained a MSc in Management from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology in 1997 under a SAF post-graduate scholarship, and attended the Advanced Management Programme at INSEAD, France in 2007. He was awarded the Public Administration Medal (Gold) (Military) in 2005, and the Long Service Medal (Military) in and attended the Advanced Management Programme at INSEAD, France in 2007. He was awarded the Public Administration Medal (Gold) (Military) in 2005, and the Long Service Medal (Military) in 2007. He also received the Medal of Commendation Award at the NTUC May Day Award 2017.

title: 0020_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_leadership_professor-chua-kee-chaing.html
text: Home About SIT Leadership Senior Management Professor Chua Kee Chaing Professor Chua Kee Chaing President, Singapore Institute of Technology Professor Chua Kee Chaing is the President, Singapore Institute of Technology (SIT). He joined SIT in September 2019 as Deputy President (Academic) and Provost. Prior to joining SIT, Prof Chua was the Dean of the Faculty of Engineering at the National University of Singapore (NUS). During his 30-year career at NUS, Prof Chua also served in other academic appointments including Vice Dean for Research at the Faculty of Engineering and Head of the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering. He also spent time outside of NUS either on secondment or leave of absence to serve as the Deputy Director of the Centre for Wireless Communications (now the Institute for Infocomm Research, A*STAR) (1995-2000), the Founding Head of the Mobile Core R&D Department at Siemens Singapore (2001-2003) and the Director of Projects at the National Research the Institute for Infocomm Research, A*STAR) (1995-2000), the Founding Head of the Mobile Core R&D Department at Siemens Singapore (2001-2003) and the Director of Projects at the National Research Foundation, Prime Minister’s Office (2006-2008). Prof Chua’s research interests are in communication networks, including wireless, optical, sensor and underwater networks. He has co-authored more than 200 internationally refereed journal and conference papers, 2 books and 5 patents in these areas. Prof Chua is a Fellow of the Singapore Academy of Engineering and the Institution of Engineers, Singapore.

title: 0021_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_leadership_professor-john-thong.html
text: Home About SIT Leadership Senior Management Professor John Thong Professor John Thong Deputy President (Academic) & Provost, Singapore Institute of Technology Professor John Thong is the Deputy President (Academic) and Provost, Singapore Institute of Technology (SIT), overseeing the university’s academic policies and curriculum, applied learning, faculty development and student administration. Prior to joining SIT in August 2020 as Vice Provost, Prof Thong was the Head of the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at the National University of Singapore (NUS). He graduated with a B.A. (1st Class Honours) in Electrical Sciences from Cambridge University in 1985, and a PhD in Electrical Engineering from the same university in 1989. In the same year, he was elected to a research fellowship at King’s College, Cambridge, where he remained until 1991 before joining NUS in 1992. He has served on various grant and oversight panels and committees for A*STAR Science and Engineering fellowship at King’s College, Cambridge, where he remained until 1991 before joining NUS in 1992. He has served on various grant and oversight panels and committees for A*STAR Science and Engineering Research Council (SERC) and the Ministry of Education’s Academic Research Fund (AcRF). He was co-director of the National Research Foundation (NRF) Quantum Engineering Programme before stepping down in August 2020. Following his PhD research, Prof Thong’s early research interests were in scanning electron microscopy and related techniques, electron-optical instruments, and electron sources. He co-founded Scanning Beam Technology Pte Ltd in 2000 to commercialise electron-beam techniques for integrated circuit analysis. His interests turned to nanotechnology in the early 2000’s, with his most recent research being focused on structural, electrical and thermal properties of 1D and 2D nanomaterials.

title: 0022_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_leadership_professor-susanna-leong.html
text: Home About SIT Leadership Senior Management Professor Susanna Leong Professor Susanna Leong Vice President (Applied Research), Singapore Institute of Technology Professor Susanna Leong is the Vice President (Applied Research) at the Singapore Institute of Technology. She oversees the development and growth of applied research, including formulating and implementing strategies, policies and processes to enhance the quality and integrity of research activities in the university. Her current research interests are in biomolecular engineering and bioprocess engineering for applications in food, healthcare and bioremediation. Her ongoing research projects are done in close partnerships with local enterprises, multinational companies and government agencies with the intent to drive translation. Prof Leong sits on several national technical panels, including Startup SG Tech and food scientific review panels. She is also an editorial board member for a number of international peer-reviewed Prof Leong sits on several national technical panels, including Startup SG Tech and food scientific review panels. She is also an editorial board member for a number of international peer-reviewed scientific journals, including ‘Chemical Engineering Science’ and ‘Food and Bioproducts Processing’. Her previous appointments at SIT include serving as Cluster Director of the Food, Chemical and Biotechnology cluster, as well as Assistant Provost (Applied Research). Her research accomplishments have been recognised through several awards, including the Tan Chin Tuan Fellowship in Engineering, most cited author for ‘Food and Bioproducts Engineering’ (2006-2009), and the Danckwerts-Pergamon Prize awarded by Cambridge University. She was a recipient of the Public Administration Medal (Bronze), National Day Award in 2018. Prof Leong is currently a Fellow of the Cambridge Commonwealth Trust. She is also an Adjunct Professor at the Department of Biochemistry and Synthetic Biology for Clinical and Day Award in 2018. Prof Leong is currently a Fellow of the Cambridge Commonwealth Trust. She is also an Adjunct Professor at the Department of Biochemistry and Synthetic Biology for Clinical and Technological Innovation (SynCTI) at the Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, National University of Singapore, as well as SIFBI, A*STAR. Prof Leong graduated with a BEng (First Class Honours) and MPhil in Chemical Engineering from the University of Manchester Institute of Science and Technology (UMIST), and a PhD in Chemical Engineering from Cambridge University.

title: 0023_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_our-history.html
text: Home About SIT Who We Are Our History Our History Singapore Institute of Technology was incorporated in 2009 to provide more opportunities for polytechnic graduates to further their education. Here are some key moments in our journey. 2025 MAY Historic Move to Punggol Campus Completed SIT welcomed 6,000 SITizens from the Engineering (ENG), Food, Chemical & Biotechnology (FCB), and Health & Social Sciences (HSS) Clusters to Punggol Campus, marking the completion of the University’s phased move to its new home. With the entire university community together under one roof for the first time in SIT's history, the move signalled the beginning of a new chapter that reinforces SIT's commitment to applied learning and to becoming the University for industry. JANUARY SIT Celebrates 10 Years of IWSP SIT's signature Integrated Work Study Programme (IWSP) turned 10. For the past decade, the IWSP has been a trailblazer, empowering SITizens to integrate theory and practice, and develop deep of IWSP SIT's signature Integrated Work Study Programme (IWSP) turned 10. For the past decade, the IWSP has been a trailblazer, empowering SITizens to integrate theory and practice, and develop deep specialist skills in their chosen field. Through training, robust mentorship, and strong industry support, SIT's IWSP has cultivated a new generation of highly sought-after professionals who are industry-ready and equipped to thrive in today's dynamic world of work. 2024 September SIT Welcomed First Batch of Students into Punggol Campus In the first phase of its move, SIT welcomed 3,800 SITizens from the Business, Communication and Design (BCD) and the Infocomm Technology (ICT) Clusters to start Academic Year 2024 at the new Punggol Campus. May Launched ‘10 Acts for Good’ Initiative The Mangrove Conservatory is the first of SIT’s ‘10 Acts for Good’, a year-long community and social initiative in celebration of its 10th anniversary as an Autonomous University. The University is embarking on Conservatory is the first of SIT’s ‘10 Acts for Good’, a year-long community and social initiative in celebration of its 10th anniversary as an Autonomous University. The University is embarking on 10 activities to give back to the community, including mentorship programmes for at-risk youth, providing healthy food options for beneficiaries within the Punggol community and the setting up of the Mangrove Conservatory. March SIT Celebrated 10 Years as an Autonomous University SIT marked a momentous occasion on 20 March with a Gala Dinner at the Conrad Orchard Hotel to celebrate its 10th anniversary as an Autonomous University (AU). The evening was filled with reminiscing about the decade of SIT’s remarkable growth and impact on Singapore's higher education landscape. Learn more about our 10th Anniversary Celebrations here . 2023 October Launched ‘Learn for Life’ Initiative to Support Lifelong Learning for All Alumni The ‘Learn for Life’ initiative was announced to enable all SIT alumni Anniversary Celebrations here . 2023 October Launched ‘Learn for Life’ Initiative to Support Lifelong Learning for All Alumni The ‘Learn for Life’ initiative was announced to enable all SIT alumni to enrol for one of SIT's Continuing Education and Training (CET) modules for free, once every five years, from 1 April 2024. They can also apply for postgraduate degree programmes through a simplified admissions process. July Launched Teaching and Learning Academy (STLA) to advance its Competency-Based Education The SIT Teaching and Learning Academy was launched at the Applied Learning Conference 2024 to drive the Recognition of Prior Learning Framework to better support in-employment learners in their upskilling or reskilling efforts. May Topping Out Ceremony for SIT Punggol Campus The Topping Out Ceremony marked a major construction milestone for SIT Punggol Campus. The campus, expected to be operational by the second half of 2024, is designed to augment teaching and learning with spaces Out Ceremony marked a major construction milestone for SIT Punggol Campus. The campus, expected to be operational by the second half of 2024, is designed to augment teaching and learning with spaces that allow stronger industry collaborations, featuring facilities for exchanges of ideas and hands-on experiential learning. 2022 November Launched Singapore’s First Competency-based Stackable Micro-credential Pathway SIT, in collaboration with three industry partners and three polytechnics, launched the Competency-based Stackable Micro-credential (CSM) pathway in November 2022. The pathway offers micro-credentials to learners who want to upskill while staying employed, enabling them to acquire new competencies to take on larger roles in their organisations or pivot to emerging technologies. The pathway was piloted through SIT’s Bachelor of Science with Honours in Applied Computing. October SIT Conferred First Honorary Graduate As part of SIT Graduation Ceremony 2022, Mr Ng Yat Chung, the pathway was piloted through SIT’s Bachelor of Science with Honours in Applied Computing. October SIT Conferred First Honorary Graduate As part of SIT Graduation Ceremony 2022, Mr Ng Yat Chung, the founding Chairman of SIT’s Board of Trustees was conferred the university’s first honorary degree of Doctor of Letters in recognition of his contributions. The ceremony also saw the graduation of SIT’s inaugural cohort of 52 students from the Air Transport Management degree programme – a first-of-its-kind offered by an autonomous university in Singapore. March Mr Bill Chang Appointed as Chairman of SIT Board of Trustees SIT welcomed Mr Bill Chang, Chief Executive Officer, Digital InfraCo, Singtel, (formerly Chief Executive Officer, Group Enterprise and Chief Executive Officer, Regional Data Centre Business of Singtel), as the new Chairman of its Board of Trustees on 16 March 2022. Mr Chang took over from Mr Ng Yat Chung, who stepped down on 15 March 2022 as founding Chairman of SIT’s Board Business of Singtel), as the new Chairman of its Board of Trustees on 16 March 2022. Mr Chang took over from Mr Ng Yat Chung, who stepped down on 15 March 2022 as founding Chairman of SIT’s Board after more than 12 years of exemplary leadership. January Professor Chua Kee Chaing Appointed as The Third President of SIT Professor Chua Kee Chaing assumed the role of SIT President, succeeding Professor Tan Thiam Soon. He joined SIT in September 2019 as Deputy President (Academic) and Provost and has been instrumental in advancing the university’s academic policies and curriculum, promoting applied learning and applied research, fostering faculty development, and enhancing student administration. 2021 Launched The Competency-Based Workplace Learning Pathway at SkillsFuture@SIT In partnership with SkillsFuture Singapore (SSG), SIT hosted SkillsFuture@SIT, a webinar centred on the theme of ‘Supporting Industry Transformation through Upskilling Singapore’s Workforce’. The event also featured SkillsFuture Singapore (SSG), SIT hosted SkillsFuture@SIT, a webinar centred on the theme of ‘Supporting Industry Transformation through Upskilling Singapore’s Workforce’. The event also featured the launch of SIT’s pilot Competency-Based Workplace Learning pathway in collaboration with Ensign InfoSecurity Pte Ltd, LTA, SBS Transit Ltd and SMRT Corporation Ltd. 2020 November Launched The National Centre of Excellence for Workplace Learning (NACE@SIT) Established in November 2020 in partnership with SkillsFuture Singapore (SSG), the National Centre of Excellence for Workplace Learning at SIT (NACE@SIT) champions workplace learning, empowering businesses with best-in-class practices, expertise, and networks. NACE@SIT plays a pivotal role in driving innovation and collaboration between academia and industry, fostering mutually beneficial partnerships, and facilitating the exchange of knowledge and skills. March Established The Applied Research Office In March 2020, the SIT Applied academia and industry, fostering mutually beneficial partnerships, and facilitating the exchange of knowledge and skills. March Established The Applied Research Office In March 2020, the SIT Applied Research Office (ARO) was established to facilitate SIT’s translational research and innovation programmes in key areas such as Energy Resilience, Sustainable Food Innovation, Field/Service Robotics Engineering, Sustainable Infrastructure Engineering & Maintenance, and Community Health Transformation. These programmes were designed to bridge the gap between academia and industry, fostering a collaborative environment that encourages innovation and addresses real-world challenges through impactful research. Launched Master’s And Doctorate Degrees By Research SIT introduced Postgraduate Programmes by Research (PGR) which included an Industrial Doctorate Programme and an Industrial Master Programme. These centres serve as collaborative hubs for technology development and innovation initiatives. September Groundbreaking Ceremony For SIT Punggol Campus On 10 September 2019, SIT held its Groundbreaking Ceremony for its future centralised campus in Punggol. Over the years, the Graduate Employment Survey (GES) has shown positive results, confirming the success of SIT’s model of applied learning. 2014 IWSP – Disrupting the Traditional Student Work Attachment Our Integrated Work Study Programme (IWSP) was launched, where students immerse themselves in a real work environment for six to 12 months (longer than a traditional internship) through work attachments with our industry partners. IWSP has was launched, where students immerse themselves in a real work environment for six to 12 months (longer than a traditional internship) through work attachments with our industry partners. IWSP has become a signature feature of SIT’s programmes, enabling students to maximise learning in an authentic work environment and hit the ground running when they enter the workforce. March Established the SITizen-DNA In the spirit of instilling a set of traits that SIT believes every SITizen should embody, the ‘SIT-DNA’ was introduced in March 2014. Now known as the ‘SITizen-DNA’, these traits are an integral part of a SITizen’s identity and continue to serve as a guiding principle in steering our curriculum design, pedagogical approach, student interaction and culture. March Minted as Singapore’s Fifth Autonomous University SIT officially become an autonomous university, granting it the same status as existing universities in Singapore. He emphasised that university education is a journey of lifelong learning that builds skills rather than pursuing paper qualifications. The saying "Once a SITizen, Always a SITizen" describes SIT’s students’ lifelong learning journey. 2010 January Inaugural Open House SIT held its inaugural Open House in 2010. The programmes have a global outlook and include an Overseas Immersion Programme for on-site learning, which enables SIT to provide specialised and industry-relevant degrees. During his tenure, SIT grew from 10 to 17 programmes with 1,000 enrolled students. September Singapore Institute of Technology (SIT) Was Established Incorporated in September 2009, the Singapore Institute of Applied Technology (SIAT) aimed to provide more education pathways for Singaporeans to obtain university education. title: 0024_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_our-mission-and-vision.html
text: Home About SIT Who We Are Our Mission and Vision Our Mission and Vision MISSION To maximise the potential of our learners and to innovate with industry, through an integrated applied learning and research approach, so as to contribute to the economy and society. VISION A Premier University of Applied Learning, recognised as Singapore’s University for Industry. SIT aspires to be a vibrant applied learning university that is well regarded in Singapore and the region for lifelong learning and nurturing career- and future-ready talent. It will be Singapore’s university for industry , providing for their innovation needs and helping them move up the innovation value chain. SIT will also be a valued partner to other institutes of higher learning (IHLs), industry, public agencies and other entities in the delivery of education and innovation services. Operationally, SIT will be a digital and data-driven university, serving all its stakeholders effectively.

title: 0025_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_our-values-and-sitizen-dna.html
text: Home About SIT Who We Are Our Values and SITizen-DNA SIT's Core Values Our core values support our vision and mission. It is the foundation of our workplace culture and reflects the behaviours that shape the way we work with one another and our industry partners. Purpose and Agility We are purposeful and united in delivering on our mission We are agile in responding to disruptions and opportunities Respect and Sustainability We respect one another and are open to a diversity of views We promote sustainability in our lifestyle and operations, education and research endeavours Integrity and Excellence We maintain a high level of professional integrity at all times We strive for excellence in all that we do The SITizen-DNA Our aim is to produce work-ready graduates armed with more than just paper qualifications, but skills that can strengthen and energise the industries and the wider community. The four key traits of the SITizen-DNA serves as guiding principles for curriculum design, paper qualifications, but skills that can strengthen and energise the industries and the wider community. The four key traits of the SITizen-DNA serves as guiding principles for curriculum design, pedagogical approach, and student interaction. Thinking Tinkerers A ‘thinking tinkerer’ is someone who is not just passionate about hands-on work, but also able to understand and apply the knowledge picked up during the process. He/She is both skilled with his/her hands and possesses theoretical knowledge. Hence, he/she is constantly looking to improve things and solve practical problems with innovative solutions. As Singapore’s economy moves up the value chain, industries will require trained manpower with applicable skills. In addition, there is also an increasing demand for workers to be able to think on their feet, apply their knowledge and tailor it to changing situations. Able to Learn, Unlearn and Relearn Someone who is able to ‘learn, unlearn and relearn’ embraces change and be able to think on their feet, apply their knowledge and tailor it to changing situations. Able to Learn, Unlearn and Relearn Someone who is able to ‘learn, unlearn and relearn’ embraces change and continues to learn beyond school. He/She knows that new knowledge opens the door to new opportunities. Hence, he/she is adaptable to change and subscribes to lifelong learning. In the last 50 years, Singapore’s economy has gone through various transformations — from labour-intensive to skills-intensive, and from capital-intensive to knowledge-intensive. Technology is advancing at an unprecedented speed of change.. To stay on top of the economy’s ever changing landscape, graduates need to constantly ‘learn, unlearn and relearn’ to stay ahead. Catalysts for Transformation A catalyst for transformation effects change by increasing efficiency and creating value through innovation. SIT graduates are trained to be ‘catalysts for transformation’. They seek to understand the organisation they are effects change by increasing efficiency and creating value through innovation. SIT graduates are trained to be ‘catalysts for transformation’. They seek to understand the organisation they are in at the process and system level, and find ways to improve and implement efficiency. They bring in new ideas and create new products or services to generate new growth pathways. To remain competitive in the disruptive economy, it is essential for businesses to adopt a highly productive mode of operation, with a focus on value-adding solutions. SIT works closely with the industry, especially with SMEs, to identify challenges and problems that our students can work on. Such win-win collaborations translate into tremendous opportunities for students as they navigate unchartered waters and grow in their respective fields. Grounded in the Community Being ‘grounded in the community’ means our students and graduates serve and strengthen the community that they are in through their skills and in their respective fields. Grounded in the Community Being ‘grounded in the community’ means our students and graduates serve and strengthen the community that they are in through their skills and knowledge. As part of the larger Singapore community, our students work together across various disciplines to engage with their communities and understand their needs, making improvements that impact the social, economic or environmental aspects of the community they live and work within.

title: 0035_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_presidents-welcome.html
text: Home About SIT Who We Are President's Welcome President's Welcome The Singapore Institute of Technology (SIT) is Singapore’s first University of Applied Learning and the third largest university by intake in Singapore. SIT’s mission is to maximise the potential of our learners and to innovate with industry, through an integrated applied learning and research approach, so as to contribute to the economy and society. The last few years have seen disruptions on the technological, social and environmental fronts. More than ever, we need to prepare students for evolving job opportunities, and for a lifetime of careers. Placing industry and individuals at the heart of SIT, we offer specialised degree programmes that prepare our graduates to be work-ready professionals and lifelong learners, contributing to and impacting both industry and society. As a pioneer in the applied learning pathway in university education in Singapore, our pedagogy embraces experiential and authentic learning – key to and impacting both industry and society. As a pioneer in the applied learning pathway in university education in Singapore, our pedagogy embraces experiential and authentic learning – key ingredients to nurture active, lifelong learners who are able to hit the ground running when they graduate. Our holistic education model not only ensures that we develop graduates with a mastery of deep disciplinary knowledge, but with relevant transferable and inter-disciplinary skills and know-how needed to function effectively and with agility in a fast-changing industry landscape. As Singapore’s premier university of applied learning, we continue to find new ways to deliver these programmes, integrating work and study in a symbiotic continuum. In research, SIT is positioned to bridge knowledge creation and utilisation. Our focus is on applied research with impact to businesses. We focus on translational research and innovation activities — where we work with other institutes of higher creation and utilisation. Our focus is on applied research with impact to businesses. We focus on translational research and innovation activities — where we work with other institutes of higher learning, research institutes and companies – to bring research results closer to end products and services. The outcomes that we seek in applied research are to help businesses build new solutions, and through these engagements, inform and sharpen our curriculum. I invite you to have a look around the university and get to know us better. Join us as a student, staff, partner or supporter, and participate in our journey as we transform individuals, industry and society. We placed emphasis on creating spaces that promote interaction and participation, through the inclusion of many big and small courtyards, with hard and soft landscapes. These conducive spaces become natural centrifugal forces that bring people together to foster an exchange of ideas and for learning to take place, even outside the classrooms. chevron--up Campus Heart About SIT Campus Heart SIT Campus Heart is a "Campus-in-a-Park" designed by WOHA Architects. Uniquely endowed with an existing secondary forest, the design capitalises on this green site asset by integrating its learning environments with biophilic indoor-outdoor tropical by WOHA Architects. Uniquely endowed with an existing secondary forest, the design capitalises on this green site asset by integrating its learning environments with biophilic indoor-outdoor tropical spaces. Principal Architect, WOHA Architects WOHA’s founding directors, Richard Hassell and Mun Summ Wong (left to right). Credits: Studio Periphery “We conserved 1.7Ha of the existing forest and designed a “Campus-in-the-Park", that is characterised by memorable place-making nodes and biophilic learning environments for students to enjoy and delight in.” - Mr Wong Mun Summ, Founding Director of WOHA To forge an evocative “Campus-in-a-Park” identity, the academic blocks are organised as a chain of buildings encompassing the central forest courtyard, which has been transformed into an accessible Community Park. SIT Campus Heart serves as the heart of SIT, contributing to a strong sense of place characterised by memorable nodes for learning, interaction, recreation and rejuvenation. You can Community Park. SIT Campus Heart serves as the heart of SIT, contributing to a strong sense of place characterised by memorable nodes for learning, interaction, recreation and rejuvenation. You can find the library, a sheltered event plaza, and the Hatchery building here. chevron--up Frequently Asked Questions chevron--up How big is the Punggol Campus? SIT’s Punggol Campus, situated within JTC’s Punggol Digital District (PDD), occupies a total land area of approximately nine hectares. It comprises two plots known as ‘SIT Campus Heart’ and ‘SIT Campus Court’. SIT Campus Heart occupies 4.9 hectares and has four buildings, while SIT Campus Court occupies 4.2 hectares and has six buildings. chevron--up What are the transport options to get to Punggol Campus? Our 'Campus-in-the-park' is a car-lite district that encourages eco-friendly commuting. Visitors are highly encouraged to use public transport to our campus. Here are the various transport options: Public Transport: Punggol Campus is district that encourages eco-friendly commuting. Visitors are highly encouraged to use public transport to our campus. Here are the various transport options: Public Transport: Punggol Campus is served by both public bus and rail options. MRT: Punggol Coast MRT Station is located at the doorstep of the campus. Please use Exit 1 for direct access. Bus: The Punggol Coast Bus Interchange, situated adjacent to the campus, is served by bus services 34 and 117/117M. Alternatively, services 84W and 84G from the Punggol Bus Interchange also provide direct access to the campus. LRT: Teck Lee LRT station is within walking distance of Punggol Campus. Pedestrians can walk via the covered linkway and through Campus Boulevard. Cars: Punggol Campus is within Punggol Digital District (PDD), a designated car-lite district. Visitors are encouraged to use public transport as parking spaces and season parking in PDD are limited. Public parking facilities are available in the vicinity. Bicycles and car-lite district. Visitors are encouraged to use public transport as parking spaces and season parking in PDD are limited. Public parking facilities are available in the vicinity. Bicycles and Personal Mobility Devices: Dedicated parking spaces and End-of-Trip (EOT) facilities, including lockers and toilets with shower facilities, are strategically located across the campus. chevron--up What are the parking charges like? Punggol Coast MRT station and the Punggol Coast Bus Interchange are within walking distance of Punggol Campus, so chartered bus services will not be provided. The public transportation options nearby aim to facilitate easy access for all students and staff commuting to the campus. chevron--up Is there an internal shuttle bus service within Punggol Campus? There are no internal shuttle services. Punggol Campus is designed for exploration on foot, featuring designated walkways and link bridges that facilitate seamless access between the two plots: SIT Campus Court and SIT Campus Heart. Punggol Campus is designed for exploration on foot, featuring designated walkways and link bridges that facilitate seamless access between the two plots: SIT Campus Court and SIT Campus Heart. chevron--up What will happen to SIT@Poly Buildings? SIT's existing campuses will gradually be returned to the authorities when they are no longer needed. chevron--up Is the Punggol Campus accessible to the public? Punggol Campus is envisioned as more than just an educational institution. It is a vibrant hub within the community. The public can access some spaces within Punggol Campus. Floors from Campus Boulevard (Level 2) and below, comprising areas such as the Community Park, Heritage Trail, Makerspace, SIT Visitor Gallery and food courts will be accessible to the public. chevron--up Do the public have access to campus facilities and amenities? SIT students and staff have priority access to the facilities and amenities at Punggol Campus. Requests by external parties will be managed on a public have access to campus facilities and amenities? SIT students and staff have priority access to the facilities and amenities at Punggol Campus. Requests by external parties will be managed on a case-by-case basis. For information on venue capacity and rates, please visit https://www.singaporetech.edu.sg/about/punggol-campus/facilities-booking . For leasing or short-term hire enquiries, contact SIT.EST.Leasing_Enquiry@singaporetech.edu.sg . chevron--up What are the facilities at the Punggol Campus which can be booked by the public? The Ho Bee Auditorium, Foyers, Seminar Rooms, Lectorials and Kwek Leng Beng University Tower Main Lobby are available for booking. For information on venue capacity and rates, please visit https://www.singaporetech.edu.sg/about/punggol-campus/facilities-booking . For leasing or short-term hire enquiries, contact SIT.EST.Leasing_Enquiry@singaporetech.edu.sg . chevron--up Are there shop spaces available for lease at the Punggol Campus? For leasing or . For leasing or short-term hire enquiries, contact SIT.EST.Leasing_Enquiry@singaporetech.edu.sg . chevron--up Are there shop spaces available for lease at the Punggol Campus? For leasing or short-term hire enquiries, contact SIT.EST.Leasing_Enquiry@singaporetech.edu.sg . chevron--up How were the needs and expectations of students and staff taken into consideration when designing the new campus? The journey towards creating our campus at Punggol was a collaborative effort from the get-go. From 2016 to 2019, we conducted numerous visioning workshops, ideation and brainstorming sessions, as well as interviews with over 500 students and staff. This inclusive approach helped shape the Punggol Campus concept plan and design requirements. The final design underwent a comprehensive review, which involved gathering feedback from key university stakeholders. This thorough and inclusive process ensured that the approved design truly reflects the collective vision and needs of our SIT community. involved gathering feedback from key university stakeholders. This thorough and inclusive process ensured that the approved design truly reflects the collective vision and needs of our SIT community. chevron--up How is the campus uniquely designed to support applied learning? Are there dedicated study areas for students? Punggol Campus provides a vibrant learning environment that underpins SIT’s applied learning pedagogy. Its location in the heart of PDD and the Punggol heartland creates many opportunities for cross-collaboration between SIT, industry and the community. Students and faculty staff are involved in applied research projects and community activities. Such collaborations enable our students to stay competitive amid the fast-evolving skill sets required by industry and groom them to seamlessly transition into their chosen careers. SIT students especially benefit from these partnerships, by leveraging the expertise of industry through ‘industry teaching laboratories’ as well to seamlessly transition into their chosen careers. SIT students especially benefit from these partnerships, by leveraging the expertise of industry through ‘industry teaching laboratories’ as well as the Living Labs. There are several dedicated spaces for students. These areas, from cosy study nooks to collaborative project zones, are specially designed to support our students’ study sessions and project work. chevron--up What types of sports facilities are there on campus? The available sporting facilities include: SIT Campus Court: Multipurpose halls (drawn courts include basketball, badminton, volleyball, netball, tchoukball and floorball), tennis courts, bouldering wall, dance studios, martial arts hall and fitness gym. chevron--up What are the retail and F&B options available on campus? The operating hours of the food courts at Punggol Campus are as follows: Foodgle Hub , SIT Campus Court, Block E4 Monday to Friday: 7.30 am to 7.30 pm Saturday, Sunday & Public Holidays: 8.30 am The operating hours of the food courts at Punggol Campus are as follows: Foodgle Hub , SIT Campus Court, Block E4 Monday to Friday: 7.30 am to 7.30 pm Saturday, Sunday & Public Holidays: 8.30 am to 7.30 pm Wholesome by Food Canopy , SIT Campus Heart, Block W3 Level 2 Monday to Friday: 7.30 am to 7:30 pm Saturday: 7.30 am to 3:00 pm Sunday & Public Holidays: Closed There are a diverse range of F&B, retail, and lifestyle options in Punggol Campus and its immediate vicinity such as Punggol Coast Mall and the Punggol Coast Hawker Centre. chevron--up Are there takeaway options at the food courts on campus? As part of our waste reduction efforts, disposable takeaway packaging will not be provided at our food courts on campus. You may either Bring Your Own cups/containers or use our Green Takeaway. At Foodgle Hub, Green Takeaway has been made available. Customers who wish to order takeaways can loan reusable cups or containers from the stallholders at a fee, which includes a refundable At Foodgle Hub, Green Takeaway has been made available. title: 0037_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_punggol-campus_explore-spaces.html
text: Home About SIT Who We Are Punggol Campus Explore Spaces Explore Spaces Mixed-use spaces for students, industry and community in Punggol. Lectorials Hybrid lecture-tutorial halls A lecture-tutorial hybrid teaching model used in our lecture halls. Referred to as the ‘flipped classroom’ approach to teaching, lectorials are large-scale classes underpinned by active student-centred learning that utilise inquiry-based learning. Students can look forward to collaborative and interactive activities in place of traditional didactic lectures and tutorials. Superlabs High-specification, flexible teaching laboratories High-specification, flexible teaching laboratories that allow multiple student groups to be taught simultaneously in the same space. Each lab bench cluster is equipped with its own AV support and teacher demonstration area, enabling different classes to run simultaneously. Library of the Future Experiential and innovative learning Our Library plays an enhanced role in being an AV support and teacher demonstration area, enabling different classes to run simultaneously. Library of the Future Experiential and innovative learning Our Library plays an enhanced role in being an extension of the classroom with a variety of spaces to foster students’ varied learning needs. It is the key central campus space supporting communal learning and knowledge access, with tinkering and innovation as central concepts. Integrated with our makerspace and collaborative spaces, the library of the future will become more of a ‘LABrary’ that facilitates experiential learning to support a culture of intellectual engagement and exchange across disciplines. Smart Playground Digital exploration for Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) The Smart Playground is a hub for STEM digital exploration. A digital interactive learning space dedicated to a sensory experience of SIT’s applied research. It is also open to the public to discover, visualise and contribute to for STEM digital exploration. A digital interactive learning space dedicated to a sensory experience of SIT’s applied research. It is also open to the public to discover, visualise and contribute to research projects. Environments will be replicated at a real-world scale, allowing the public to experience real project scenarios in the digital world as ‘citizen scientists’. title: 0041_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_about_punggol-campus_redefining.html
text: Home About SIT Who We Are Punggol Campus Redefining Education Redefining Education The SIT Punggol Campus sets the stage for the University’s growth to become a premier University of Applied Learning, recognised as Singapore’s University for Industry. Nestled within the vibrant Punggol Digital District, SIT’s co-location with industry and community will allow students, academia, industry and the public to intermingle seamlessly. The new campus will be a hub for knowledge, collaboration, innovation and test-bedding. It boasts several Living Labs that enrich the authenticity of students' learning experiences and provide fertile ground for impactful applied research. Looking ahead, SIT remains steadfast in its commitment to excellence, innovation and inclusivity. Punggol offers SIT the opportunity to closely integrate with the community, enhancing STEM outreach to inspire and nurture young students. The University aims to cultivate students who are grounded in the community to drive to closely integrate with the community, enhancing STEM outreach to inspire and nurture young students. The University aims to cultivate students who are grounded in the community to drive positive change. Dedicated to pushing boundaries and embracing change, SIT continues to chart new frontiers in higher education and nurture the next generation of game changers. Applied Learning Learning opportunities everywhere Designed to support diverse learning styles for specialised programmes, the University provides a dynamic environment catered for the digital era. Some facilities include a Library of the future, Superlabs, Lectorials and a Smart Playground. Library of the Future Experiential Learning Learn More Superlabs High-specification, flexible teaching laboratories Learn More Lectorials Hybrid lecture-tutorial halls Learn More Smart Playground Digital exploration for Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) Learn More ❮ ❯ Industry Partnership Visible industry Hybrid lecture-tutorial halls Learn More Smart Playground Digital exploration for Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) Learn More ❮ ❯ Industry Partnership Visible industry collaboration We are connected to JTC Corporation’s business park via Campus Boulevard and the elevated Collaboration Loop. Students and faculty can test-bed proofs-of-concept across the street, while industry partners innovate at The Hatchery on campus. Vibrant community We welcome everyone We are accessible to the public via the Punggol Coast MRT station and will have facilities for everyone to enjoy. The public can explore the campus on foot or cycle down the main Campus Boulevard — an 800m-long walkway connecting the whole district. There is also the 1.3km green Heritage Trail, a forest conservation project along the old Punggol Road that now links Punggol Waterway Park to Punggol Promenade park connector. Other facilities include dining and shopping options at the Market Village by the project along the old Punggol Road that now links Punggol Waterway Park to Punggol Promenade park connector. title: 0043_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_academic-bulletin_academic-matters.html
text: Home Academic Bulletin Academic Matters Academic Matters This section of the Bulletin covers general academic guidelines and procedures for learners across all our degree, graduate diploma and certificate programmes. Additional information relevant to each programme can be found in each programme-specific section of this Bulletin. Academic Calendar chevron--up Academic Calendar Each Academic Year typically starts in late August or early September and ends in August of the following year, and comprises three trimesters. View the academic calendar for undergraduate programmes . View the academic calendar for postgraduate programmes . General Information about our Programmes chevron--up Components of a Programme of Study A programme component may either be a module or a competency unit that a learner undertakes to fulfil the requirements of a programme of study. A programme component may also refer to a micro-credential, which is an 18-credit coherent block of learning and may be that a learner undertakes to fulfil the requirements of a programme of study. A programme component may also refer to a micro-credential, which is an 18-credit coherent block of learning and may be competency-based or non-competency-based. Competency-based micro-credentials comprise competency units, while non-competency-based micro-credentials comprise modules. A credit is a unit measure of a learner’s workload in each module or competency unit where one credit equates to 25 notional hours of contact time, projects, practical work, self-study, continual assessments (CA) and/or examination. Learners undertaking competency units will need to accord additional practice time to demonstrate the required competencies through one or more assessments. Modules/competency units are coded in the form "ABC W XYZ C ", where: S/N Module Code Competency Unit Code 1 “ABC” is a 3-letter prefix that denotes the programme and/or discipline “ABC” is a 3-letter prefix that denotes the cluster under which "ABC W XYZ C ", where: S/N Module Code Competency Unit Code 1 “ABC” is a 3-letter prefix that denotes the programme and/or discipline “ABC” is a 3-letter prefix that denotes the cluster under which the competency unit is offered. 2 “ W ” is a number indicating the module level “ W ” is a number indicating the competency unit level 3 “XYZ” is the module number “XYZ” is the competency unit number, with the first digit "X" denoting the programme/discipline 4 Any appropriate suffix to denote different pathway modules. “ C ” stands for these components are delivered through the competency-based education pedagogy The table below provides descriptions of the digits representing the module level. Module Level Codes Description Level 1 Introductory modules that cover fundamental concepts and skills to build strong foundation for undergraduate learners. Level 2 Intermediate modules that cover deeper knowledge in the discipline area and/or develop broader level skills for undergraduate and skills to build strong foundation for undergraduate learners. Level 3 Modules that cover specific topics which allow undergraduate learners to explore areas of interest within or across discipline. Micro-modules (MMs) in SIT have specific definitions as outlined below. SIT MMs are non-credit bearing . These MMs are designed to be small, bite-sized, focused learning units or components designed to deliver specific content in a concise manner suitable for asynchronous learning. Each MM should have no more than 25 notional hours of engagement for the learners. SIT MMs are assigned a MM code in the format MMCXXXYYYY, where: (a) The first two characters “MM” denotes that this component is a have no more than 25 notional hours of engagement for the learners. SIT MMs are assigned a MM code in the format MMCXXXYYYY, where: (a) The first two characters “MM” denotes that this component is a micro-module; (b) The third character “C”* is an additional filler; (c) “XXX” represents the Cluster or Division mounting the micro-module; (d) “YYYY” is a running order number starting from 1001. * Note : Prior to AY2025/26, the third character in the MM code was either “C” or “O”, where “C” denotes “within curriculum”, and “O” denotes “outside curriculum”. With effect from AY2025/26, all new MMs will only have “C” in the third character of their MM codes. Completed MMs will be shown in learners’ Record of Achievement (ROA). Learners who are unable to attain the minimum grade required to clear the module/competency unit and/or specific programme requirements following repeat assessment attempts will be required to repeat the module or re-attempt the competency unit on the Programme Leader’s discretion. More information regarding enrolment is available under the Module and Micro-credential Enrolment section . The grade point for a module/competency unit following a repeat assessment attempt administered after results release will be capped as specified under the SIT Grading System section . Repeat Module or Re-attempt of a Competency Unit Learners of undergraduate programmes who have obtained an F, D, D+ or IC grade for any module/competency unit programme requirements may choose to . Repeat Module or Re-attempt of a Competency Unit Learners of undergraduate programmes who have obtained an F, D, D+ or IC grade for any module/competency unit programme requirements may choose to repeat/re-attempt the entire module/competency unit with approval from the respective Programme Leader and/or OU Degree Programme Director. If such module/competency unit is part of a micro-credential, the learner may register for the same micro-credential in the trimester it is next offered to repeat or re-attempt the affected module/competency unit. The grades and credits of the modules/competency units that learners had passed in that micro-credential will be carried over. For the postgraduate programmes, learners who obtained the grades listed in the table in the Repeat Assessment sub-section may choose to repeat the affected modules with approval from their Programme Leaders. In the case of repeating/re-attempting a module or competency unit, learners will be billed according to the may choose to repeat the affected modules with approval from their Programme Leaders. In the case of repeating/re-attempting a module or competency unit, learners will be billed according to the prevailing credit charging. The grade point for the repeat module/competency unit will be capped as specified under the SIT Grading System section . Leave of Absence and Withdrawal chevron--up Leave of Absence Learners who require a temporary break from their programmes can apply for Leave of Absence (LOA), subject to the maximum candidature of their programme. With effect from Academic Year 2024/25, all LOAs (including those due to personal or medical reasons) will count towards the learners’ candidature period in a programme. LOAs due to medical reasons applied before 2 September 2024 will not count towards the candidature period. Learners may apply for a maximum of three trimesters of absence in each LOA application. Applications must be made by the end of Week 2 of the affected trimester, towards the candidature period. Learners may apply for a maximum of three trimesters of absence in each LOA application. Applications must be made by the end of Week 2 of the affected trimester, otherwise tuition fees for the trimester will be charged. Applications received after Week 7 will only take effect in the subsequent trimester upon approval. Further details on Academic Penalty and Fees for Undergraduate / Postgraduate Programmes are available in the Student Intranet (SIT log-in credentials are required). First-year and re-admitted learners may not take Leave of Absence during their first trimester of study, unless they have an unforeseen medical reason. Learners pursuing any degree via the Competency-Based Stackable Micro-Credential pathway may email their LOA applications to the Registrar’s Office (email to Registrar@SingaporeTech.edu.sg ) before the start of the first affected trimester. For other learners, please refer to LOA application procedures and guidelines in the Registrar’s Office (email to Registrar@SingaporeTech.edu.sg ) before the start of the first affected trimester. For other learners, please refer to LOA application procedures and guidelines in the Student Intranet (SIT log-in credentials are required). chevron--up Withdrawal from Programme Learners who do not wish to continue their studies at SIT may submit an application to withdraw from their programme. Learners enrolled in a degree or graduate diploma programme may do so via IN4SIT (SIT log-in credentials are required). Learners enrolled in a degree programme delivered via the Competency-based Stackable Micro-credential pathway or micro-credential may contact the Registrar’s Office (email to Registrar@SingaporeTech.edu.sg ) by the end of Week 2 of the trimester, if they wish to withdraw from the programme. Applications must be made by the end of Week 2 of the affected trimester, otherwise trimester fees will be charged. Learners who apply for Programme Withdrawal from SIT after from the programme. Applications must be made by the end of Week 2 of the affected trimester, otherwise trimester fees will be charged. Learners who apply for Programme Withdrawal from SIT after Friday of Week 13 will be subject to grading for enrolled modules/competency units. SIT reserves the right to administratively withdraw any learners from their programme if they are absent from classes without prior approval for an extended period and are uncontactable. Additional information about withdrawal procedures and guidelines is available in the Student Intranet (SIT log-in credentials are required). The impact of withdrawals on trimester fees and module grading is available under the Module Registration section in the Student Intranet (SIT log-in credentials are required). Learners are advised to read this before applying for withdrawal. Conferment and Convocation chevron--up Conferment of SIT Qualifications SIT will confer the relevant qualifications on learners who fulfil the are advised to read this before applying for withdrawal. Conferment and Convocation chevron--up Conferment of SIT Qualifications SIT will confer the relevant qualifications on learners who fulfil the requirements of their programmes, in accordance with the SINGAPORE INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY ACT 2014. SIT maintains the quality of awards through stringent checks before conferment of such qualifications. To be eligible to graduate, learners must have completed the requirements for their enrolled programmes. Accordingly, the learners’ results and completion status must have been approved by the SIT Board of Examiners (BOE) or the SIT-Overseas University Joint BOE. Learners with ongoing disciplinary investigations or proceedings will have their degree or graduate diploma conferment, or certificates withheld until the resolution of the disciplinary investigations or proceedings. chevron--up Rescission of Degrees, Graduate Diplomas and Certificates The authority for rescission of a degree, withheld until the resolution of the disciplinary investigations or proceedings. chevron--up Rescission of Degrees, Graduate Diplomas and Certificates The authority for rescission of a degree, graduate diploma or certificate is the same authority for the conferment of that same qualification. Learners who are found guilty of disciplinary misconduct may have their degrees, graduate diplomas or certificates withheld, or rescinded, on the recommendation from the SIT Board of Discipline (BOD). chevron--up Posthumous Conferment of Award of SIT Qualification BOE may recommend the conferment of award of a qualification posthumously, should the learner pass away during their candidature. chevron--up Convocation SIT Convocation ceremonies are held annually for individuals who have been conferred degrees and graduate diplomas.
