title: 0616_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_business-communication-and-design_applied-research.html
text: Home Business, Communication and Design Applied Research Applied Research Co-designing solutions with industry partners Our applied research approach aims to create and capture value for our industry partners. To foster greater strategic partnerships that involve multidisciplinary applied research collaborations, talent development, as well as workforce training, SIT has set up various thematic centres to support industry in their respective needs. Design Factory@SIT Adopting a user-centric model, Design Factory@SIT (DF@SIT) seeks to support industry partners through collaborative innovation projects, thereby creating authentic interdisciplinary and collaborative learning opportunities for students, and enhance the workplace learning continuum. text: Home Business, Communication and Design About Us Our Directors' Message Associate Professor Koh Sze Kee Cluster Director Business, Communication and Design Welcome to the Business, Communication, and Design (BCD) cluster, where creativity meets innovation, and the business world comes to life! Our Cluster provides a dynamic learning environment where aspiring professionals transform into industry leaders, and where research ideas translate into solutions to shape the future of business, communication and design. We believe in fostering a vibrant community that celebrates the confluence of business acumen, effective communication, and design innovation. Our programmes are designed to empower students with knowledge and skills to excel in today’s business landscape. Whether you aspire to shape the future of global enterprises, craft compelling narratives, or create visually stunning experiences, we have the resources and expertise to guide you on your journey. Our experienced and to shape the future of global enterprises, craft compelling narratives, or create visually stunning experiences, we have the resources and expertise to guide you on your journey. Our experienced and renowned faculty members and professional officers actively engage in numerous research initiatives and interdisciplinary projects. They possess a diverse range of expertise in applied research and are dedicated to creating an environment that fosters intellectual curiosity, collaborative inquiry, and a relentless pursuit of translatable research outcomes and knowledge. As you explore our Cluster website, you will discover that we value diverse perspectives and collaborative efforts because they are essential in addressing the complex challenges of today and shaping a brighter future. Be a part of our community as we join hands to push the boundaries of education and research in the dynamic realms of business, communication and design. Associate Professor Lee Kuan-Huei Director of Be a part of our community as we join hands to push the boundaries of education and research in the dynamic realms of business, communication and design. Associate Professor Lee Kuan-Huei Director of Programmes Business, Communication and Design The Business, Communication and Design (BCD) cluster offers seven degree programmes, including Accountancy, Aviation Management, Communication and Digital Media, Hospitality and Tourism Management, Business and Infocomm Technology, AND two Food Business Management degree programmes from our overseas university partner - Culinary Institute of America. BCD students gain interdisciplinary knowledge, enabling them to work across various sectors. Each degree programme’s Industry Advisory Committee (IAC) comprises esteemed business leaders who regularly meet with faculty to ensure the curriculum remains current and industry-relevant. With the rapid development of technologies, students are required to have a foundational understanding of digital meet with faculty to ensure the curriculum remains current and industry-relevant. With the rapid development of technologies, students are required to have a foundational understanding of digital skills. We also focus on sustainability, encouraging students to grasp its impact, explore potential solutions, and implement green practices. Entrepreneurial knowledge empowers students to explore new horizons and navigate each step of the entrepreneurial process. Beyond their primary studies, students collaborate with peers from other academic clusters on projects related to design and social context. This applied learning environment fosters experimentation and learning from mistakes while engaging with academics, industry professionals, and peers. The Integrated Work Study Programme (IWSP) in the last year of the curriculum exposes students directly to the industry, allowing them to apply their knowledge in their specialisations. Before completing their studies, students undertake a in the last year of the curriculum exposes students directly to the industry, allowing them to apply their knowledge in their specialisations. Before completing their studies, students undertake a Capstone project guided by faculty members, linking theoretical knowledge with practical application to address industry challenges. Armed with substantial industry experience, our academic staff are dedicated to helping students connect with the industry. Beyond traditional classroom teaching, our approach includes real case projects, competitions, industry partner talks, simulations, and field trips. The BCD cluster warmly welcomes students who are curious, open to challenges, possess a team-oriented spirit, and demonstrate perseverance to be part of our SITizen community.

title: 0620_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_campus-locations.html
text: Home About SIT Campus Locations Campus Locations As of 5 May 2025, all five clusters and the University's corporate office have moved to Punggol Campus. SIT's existing campuses will gradually be returned to the authorities when they are no longer needed. Punggol Campus will eventually host all programmes except those offered by The Culinary Institute of America, Singapore (CIA SG). CIA SG will continue to operate from its current location at Temasek Polytechnic. Punggol Campus Address 1 Punggol Coast Road Singapore 828608 Explore Our Punggol Campus Getting to Punggol Campus Our 'Campus-in-the-park' will be a car-lite district that encourages eco-friendly commuting. Visitors are highly encouraged to use public transport to our campus. Here are the various transport options: chevron--up Public Transport MRT: Punggol Coast MRT Station is located at the doorstep of the campus. Please use Exit 1 for direct access. Bus: The Punggol Coast Bus Interchange, situated adjacent to the campus, is Transport MRT: Punggol Coast MRT Station is located at the doorstep of the campus. Please use Exit 1 for direct access. Bus: The Punggol Coast Bus Interchange, situated adjacent to the campus, is served by bus services 34 and 117/117M. Alternatively, services 84W and 84G from the Punggol Bus Interchange also provide direct access to the campus. LRT: Teck Lee LRT station is within walking distance of Punggol Campus. Pedestrians can walk via the covered linkway and through Campus Boulevard. chevron--up Cars Punggol Campus is within Punggol Digital District (PDD), a designated car-lite district. Visitors are encouraged to use public transport as parking spaces and season parking in PDD are limited. Public parking facilities are available in the vicinity. chevron--up Bicycles and Personal Mobility Devices Dedicated parking spaces and End-of-Trip (EOT) facilities, including lockers and toilets with shower facilities, are strategically located across the campus.

text: Home Research Centre for Infrastructure and Tunnel Engineering Centre for Infrastructure and Tunnel Engineering Building Industry Competency and Competitiveness in Infrastructure and Tunnel Engineering Established in partnership with Land Transport Authority (LTA), the Centre for Infrastructure and Tunnel Engineering (CITE) will serve as a platform for the civil engineering community to exchange and deepen their knowledge and skill sets in this field. The Centre will also conduct and facilitate applied research to augment safety standards in the design, construction, operation and maintenance of tunnels, and other civil engineering infrastructure. What We Do Advance the adoption of technology for transport infrastructure development including both aboveground and underground infrastructural works such as bridges, tunnels, substructures and underground structures in design, construction and maintenance. text: Home CLASIC Current Projects CLASIC Current Projects Current Projects Tools for Story-Reading / Telling for students with Special Needs This cross-cluster, collaborative project aims to develop a mobile prototype application that performs storytelling in an interactive and gamified manner for children with special needs. Unlike commercially available storytelling apps for children, the narratives in this storytelling app reference familiar sights and scenarios in the Singapore context. Backdrops for these scenarios include kopitiams, mom-and-pop shops, and void decks; while story plots include local festivals and visiting grandparents to pay respect to them on these special days, living in Housing and Development Board (HDB) flats, or playing at the void deck. A total of 11 stories of varying levels of difficulty were created, with two selected for the prototype. The storytelling app will be designed with these needs in mind to facilitate both teacher-directed and learner-directed varying levels of difficulty were created, with two selected for the prototype. The storytelling app will be designed with these needs in mind to facilitate both teacher-directed and learner-directed vocabulary learning and assessment. Additionally, the personalised and intelligent app will record and track the learner’s responses to vocabulary questions, as well as diagnose his receptive vocabulary via a digitalised Peabody Picture Vocabulary test Faculty from the Centre for Professional Communication (CPC), namely, Assoc Prof Radhika Jaidev, Asst Prof Kenneth Ong, Asst Prof Lee Hwee Hoon and Senior Lecturer Erick Tan as well as faculty from the ICT cluster, namely, Asst Prof Muhammad Fauzi Abbas and Asst Prof Alex Qiang have collaborated with speech therapists / interventionists from AWWA School to identify the needs of such children and to design and develop the story plots. Interactive Carbon Footprint Calculator Prototype for Visitors’ Journey to Mandai Wildlife Reserve (MWR) Parks In Singapore, Mandai Wildlife Reserves (MWR) is a popular destination for both domestic and international leisure visitors. It is home to wildlife parks including the Singapore Zoo, River Wonders, Night Safari, and Bird Paradise. Given the popularity of MWR parks and the completion of the Mandai Rejuvenation Project, the number of local and international visitors to this destination is expected to grow. While welcoming and offering a satisfactory experience for visitors is important, promoting sustainability in MWR parks is essential. The project team, led by Assoc Prof Michael Alexander Kruesi (from the Business, Communication and Design Cluster) comprising three SIT faculty and seven SIT students, will investigate a specific current sustainable development effort in MWR parks. This Kruesi (from the Business, Communication and Design Cluster) comprising three SIT faculty and seven SIT students, will investigate a specific current sustainable development effort in MWR parks. This project seeks to design and test-run an interactive carbon footprint calculator prototype to document and examine visitors’ carbon footprint and relevant travel behaviours to and within MWR parks. Developing Digital Reminder for the Management of Community-Dwelling Individuals at Risk of Pressure Injuries Pressure injuries (PI) are largely preventable by maintaining the quality and standard of key evidence-based practices such as risk assessment, repositioning, or turning, using pressure-relief devices; maintaining adequate nutrition and moisture; and education on PI prevention. Currently, there are no digital reminders in the market targeting the management of PI prevention. The project team, led by Assoc Prof Elaine Siow from the Health and Social Sciences Cluster, comprising three SIT are no digital reminders in the market targeting the management of PI prevention. The project team, led by Assoc Prof Elaine Siow from the Health and Social Sciences Cluster, comprising three SIT faculty and four SIT students, will develop an application software that will remind the caregivers via a tablet device at home. The application software may potentially include other functions to support caregivers at home, e.g., reminders to administer medication. The project will obtain user inputs from key stakeholders, such as caregivers and community nurses on the interface and function of this application before fabricating the first prototype. Developing and Setting Up an Interactive Health Zone in St Luke's ElderCare’s New Centre at Anchorvale PSt Luke’s ElderCare (SLEC) is a healthcare provider committed to meeting the evolving needs of their patients, clients and caregivers. SLEC delivers a full range of integrated services such as maintenance day care, day rehabilitation programme committed to meeting the evolving needs of their patients, clients and caregivers. SLEC delivers a full range of integrated services such as maintenance day care, day rehabilitation programme and dementia care. The project team, led by Senior Professional Officer Seah Jianxing, comprising four SIT Professional Officers and 50 SIT students, collaborated with SLEC to develop and set up an Interactive Health Zone in the new senior activities centre at Anchorvale, to benefit Active Agers (60 years old and above) through interactive activities. The SIT Bursary, valued at $3,000 each, is one of the most accessible financial aid instruments at SIT that is open to financially disadvantaged students (Singapore Citizens and Permanent Residents) who come from households with a monthly per capita income of no more than $850. chevron--up Why should I give? text: Home Community Engagement A LEGACY TO KEEP, A FUTURE TO BUILD A LEGACY TO KEEP, A FUTURE TO BUILD Understanding Sustainability and Our Role in Shaping Tomorrow with A/Prof Ethan Chong Discover how global warming and environmental issues affect us and the generations to come Understand what sustainable development truly means in practice Learn how individuals and businesses can take concrete steps to make a difference Explore how education, research, and industry collaboration drive impactful solutions This session will highlight the urgency of sustainability and its relevance to everyday life. Participants will gain clarity on what sustainability encompasses, why it matters for our collective future, and how small actions at the individual and organisational level can scale into meaningful impact. Through relatable insights, forward-looking strategies, and practical examples, the sharing will inspire participants to view themselves as stewards of a shared legacy, one we borrow from impact. Through relatable insights, forward-looking strategies, and practical examples, the sharing will inspire participants to view themselves as stewards of a shared legacy, one we borrow from future generations. Saturday, 25 Oct 2025 | 10am – 11.30am SIT Punggol Campus (Campus Heart) W3, Level 2, SR08 Meet Our Speaker A/Prof Ethan Chong Dr Ethan Chong leads sustainability education and research at SIT as Head of Sustainability (Education and Research). He works closely with industry to co-develop solutions and talent through training, consultation, and R&D. Beyond academia, he serves as a District Councillor, championing a liveable and sustainable Singapore for future generations. title: 0631_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_community-engagement_raise-thought-leadership-speaker-series_parental-mediation-empowering-parents-digital-world.html
text: Home Community Engagement Parental Mediation: Empowering Parents in a Digital World Parental Mediation Empowering Parents in a Digital World In today’s connected world, parents often grapple with questions such as: Should I give my child a handphone? Is it appropriate for my preschooler to use my tablet? How can I manage my children’s screen time and device use in a healthy way? This session will equip parents with practical strategies and insights to guide their children’s digital journey — from preschoolers taking their first steps with technology, to teenagers navigating the complex online landscape. Drawing on research and real-life case studies, A/Prof Jiow Hee Jhee and Ms Adeline Tay will share how parents can strike a balance between setting boundaries and empowering their children to become responsible, discerning digital users. Parents will learn how to establish positive media habits, foster open communication, and transform technology into a tool that strengthens rather to become responsible, discerning digital users. Parents will learn how to establish positive media habits, foster open communication, and transform technology into a tool that strengthens rather than disrupts family relationships. Saturday, 27 Sept 2025 | 10am – 11.30am SIT Punggol Campus (Campus Heart) W1, Level 3, Connexion Hall 2 Meet Our Speaker A/Prof Jiow Hee Jhee A/Prof Jiow Hee Jhee, SIT Deputy Director, specializes in media impact, cyber wellness, and education, with extensive experience in research, teaching, and leadership roles, shaping applied learning and digital literacy. Ms. Adeline Tay Adeline Tay, a Digital Communications graduate, advocates for children's well-being in the digital age. She educates parents on effective digital device mediation through talks, conferences, and academic research. title: 0632_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_company-sponsorship-terms-conditions.html
text: Home Company Sponsorship Terms & Conditions KEY CONDITIONS OF FUNDING BETWEEN SPONSORING ORGANISATION AND SINGAPORE INSTITUTE OF TECHNOLOGY (SIT) The Sponsoring Organisation has read and agreed to the following Key Conditions for Funding (the “Key Conditions”) between themselves and SIT: DEFINITIONS In this context, the following definition shall apply: Fees – Refers collectively to both Tuition Fees and Miscellaneous Fees as defined herein. Learner – the employee under the Sponsoring Organisation that has been enroled into the relevant degree programme via the Competency-based Stackable Micro-credential (CSM) pathway. Micro-credential – A 18-credit block of learning that can stack up to a full Bachelor or Masters qualification, aimed at helping learners acquire new skills and knowledge for their work. Miscellaneous Fees – Fees to meet part of the cost of providing insurance, learner programmes, and activities that are not covered by the Tuition Fee and grants. Miscellaneous Fees are their work. Miscellaneous Fees – Fees to meet part of the cost of providing insurance, learner programmes, and activities that are not covered by the Tuition Fee and grants. Miscellaneous Fees are payable at the beginning of each trimester and are non-refundable. Sponsoring Organisation – the company, organisation or employer that financially supports or covers the cost of Tuition Fees and Miscellaneous Fees for its Learner(s) to attend SIT’s CSM programmes. Tuition Fees – Fees payable by the Sponsoring Organisation for the Learner’s enrolment and access to SIT’s CSM programmes. PAYMENT AND BILLING The Sponsoring Organisation will sponsor Tuition Fees in entirety (i.e. 100% of Tuition Fees) for their enrolled Learner(s) (less applicable government subsidies/grant), including any Miscellaneous Fees incurred by their Learner(s). Fees are based on SIT’s prevailing rates and policies and subject to change without notice. Such Fees and related subsidies are also subject to the Ministry of Fees incurred by their Learner(s). Fees are based on SIT’s prevailing rates and policies and subject to change without notice. Such Fees and related subsidies are also subject to the Ministry of Education’s (MOE) prevailing policies. The amount of Tuition Fees payable during each academic trimester also depends on the number of credits taken by the Learner in that academic trimester. Tuition Fees and Miscellaneous Fees are reviewed periodically and may be revised by SIT. Fees are payable in Singapore (S$) dollars, and GST will be charged in relation to the fees of each Learner where applicable. SIT will invoice the Sponsoring Organisation three (3) times annually based on SIT’s billing schedule that is usually set at week 3 or 4 from start of the academic trimester. The Sponsoring Organisation shall make payment within thirty (30) days of SIT’s tax invoice date. SIT reserves the final right to deny the Learner further services if fees are not paid by the due date. The Sponsoring shall make payment within thirty (30) days of SIT’s tax invoice date. SIT reserves the final right to deny the Learner further services if fees are not paid by the due date. The Sponsoring Organisation shall provide notice of any changes in billing instructions in accordance with the provisions set out in these Key Conditions. The written notice to SIT should be sent to no less than thirty (30) days before the start of the relevant academic trimester. If the Sponsoring Organisation fails to comply with the specified notice requirements, the Sponsoring Organisation shall remain responsible for all accrued fees based on SIT’s prevailing rates and policies. If any Learner fails to complete a Micro-credential and has to re-take the same Micro-credential at a subsequent time period, the Tuition Fees and/or Miscellaneous Fees for the Micro-credential will be billed to the Sponsoring Organisation. TERMINATION OR WITHDRAWAL In the event that the Sponsoring Organisation terminates its Tuition Fees and/or Miscellaneous Fees for the Micro-credential will be billed to the Sponsoring Organisation. TERMINATION OR WITHDRAWAL In the event that the Sponsoring Organisation terminates its sponsorship for any of its Learner(s) prior to graduation, the Sponsoring Organisation shall provide written notice to SIT no less than thirty (30) days before the start of the relevant academic trimester. Within fourteen (14) calendar days from the start of SIT’s academic trimester and prior to graduation, if any Learner: (a) withdraws from the CSM programme or resigns from the Sponsoring Organisation; or (b) has selected one or more Micro-credential(s) offered by SIT under the CSM programme but subsequently withdraws from the aforesaid Micro-credential(s), SIT will not include the Tuition fees for the affected Learner in the billing to the Sponsoring Organisation for the relevant academic trimester. After more than fourteen (14) calendar days from the start of SIT’s academic trimester and for the affected Learner in the billing to the Sponsoring Organisation for the relevant academic trimester. After more than fourteen (14) calendar days from the start of SIT’s academic trimester and prior to graduation, if any Learner: (a) withdraws from the CSM programme or resigns from the Sponsoring Organisation; or (b) has selected one or more Micro-credential(s) offered by SIT under the CSM programme but subsequently withdraws from the said Micro-credential(s), the Sponsoring Organisation will be liable for any and all accrued fees (including tuition fees for any aforesaid Micro-credential(s)) set forth in the Sponsorship Letter in respect of the affected Learner’s studies at SIT, based on SIT’s prevailing rates and policies. The Sponsoring Organisation will still be liable for Miscellaneous Fees if (i) the Learner withdraws or apply for a leave of absence from SIT after the end of week 2 of their relevant trimester, (ii) if the Learner’s candidature has been terminated, or (iii) Fees if (i) the Learner withdraws or apply for a leave of absence from SIT after the end of week 2 of their relevant trimester, (ii) if the Learner’s candidature has been terminated, or (iii) in the situation if the Learner is on ‘active’ status with no modules to take. GENERAL SIT reserves the right to amend these Key Conditions, as well as its policies and processes without prior notice, effective upon publication on its website. While the CSM programme is designed for educational, learning and training purposes, SIT does not guarantee specific results, examination success, mastery of disciplines or skills, or other tangible or intangible outcomes. SIT will not be held liable for any claims, losses, and damages incurred or sustained by the Learners and/or Sponsoring Organisation related to the Learner’s application or CSM programme attendance, except in cases of SIT’s own gross negligence or wilful misconduct. Unless otherwise specified, the Learners are to adhere to SIT’s Student to the Learner’s application or CSM programme attendance, except in cases of SIT’s own gross negligence or wilful misconduct. Unless otherwise specified, the Learners are to adhere to SIT’s Student Conduct upon course enrolment and to acknowledge that there may be consequences for breaching such codes/rules. SIT reserves the right to take appropriate actions, including informing law enforcement authorities of serious violations, and imposing suspension or blacklisting Learners in cases of misconduct, transgression, or infractions of a disciplinary nature or breaches occurring during participation in the CSM programme. In such cases, the Sponsoring Organisation acknowledges that they may still be liable for Tuition Fees and/or Miscellaneous Fees. These Key Conditions are governed by Singapore law, and the courts of Singapore have exclusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising out of or relating to them. If any court finds that any provisions in these Key Conditions (or any part law, and the courts of Singapore have exclusive jurisdiction over any disputes arising out of or relating to them. If any court finds that any provisions in these Key Conditions (or any part thereof) is invalid, illegal or unenforceable, that provision or part shall, to the extent required, be deemed to be deleted, and the validity and enforceability of the other provisions herein shall not be affected.

Such reporting is essential for maintaining a safe work environment, safeguarding occupants, and preventing future incidents. Examples of incident or near-miss reporting include: Incident : A worker slips and falls on a wet floor, resulting in a sprained ankle. Near Miss : A worker slips on a wet floor but manages to regain balance and avoid falling. Incident : A piece of equipment malfunctions, causing minor burns to a worker. Near Miss : A worker notices a malfunctioning piece of equipment and reports it before anyone is injured. Incident : A worker is exposed to hazardous chemicals due to improper handling, resulting in skin irritation. Near Miss : A worker almost spills a container of hazardous chemicals but manages to prevent it from happening. Hazard reporting is essential in identifying and documenting potential risks or hazardous conditions in the workplace. Such reporting could proactively address these risks and prevent accidents, injuries, and illnesses. Examples of hazard or unsafe condition reporting include: Physical Hazard: A report of a loose handrail on a staircase that poses a falling hazard. Chemical Hazard: A report of a spill of a hazardous chemical substance in a laboratory. Biological Hazard: A report of mould growth in a building that could lead to respiratory issues. Unsafe Condition: A report of a wet floor without a warning sign, posing a slip hazard. A report of a malfunctioning machine or equipment that could cause injury to operators. text: Home About SIT Convocation 2024 Our Sponsors Acknowledgements: Sponsors (Bonded Scholarships) Through generous contributions from our industry partners, government agencies and organisations, our students have been able to receive various forms of scholarships or sponsorships to pursue their degree programmes. text: Home About SIT Convocation 2024 Ceremony Day Ceremony Day Things to note for Graduands and Guests: Arrival Graduands and guests are advised to arrive at least 1 hour before the ceremony . Please take note of the timings below. Mandatory bag checks will be conducted before you enter the venue for security purposes. Food, drinks, flowers and balloons will not be allowed into the venue. Graduands must be fully robed before entering the Theatre. Morning Ceremonies Afternoon Ceremonies Evening Ceremonies Activity Remarks 8:30 AM 1:00 PM 5:00 PM Arrival of Graduands and Guests Entry to the venue is via the Theatre Main Entrance, Level 3 Graduands are required to register your attendance at the Graduand Registration Counter. Please present your SIT matriculation card and Graduand Admission Card. Graduands who do not register their attendance at the counter will not be presented on stage. 9:00 AM 1:30 PM 5:30 PM Theatre doors will open for seating 9:15 AM 1:45 PM 5:45 PM Graduands’ Briefing Graduands who do not register their attendance at the counter will not be presented on stage. 9:00 AM 1:30 PM 5:30 PM Theatre doors will open for seating 9:15 AM 1:45 PM 5:45 PM Graduands’ Briefing in Theatre 9:30 AM 2:00 PM 6:00 PM All guests to be seated Please be punctual as the ceremony will commence on time and latecomers will not be allowed into the venue. 10:00 AM 2:30 PM 6:30 PM Start of Ceremony Admission and Seating Entry to the Theatre is strictly by Graduand Admission Card and e-tickets (for guests) only. Each registered graduand is allocated a seat number in the Theatre which is indicated on the Graduand Admission Card. As graduands are presented according to a numbered sequence, you are to refrain from swapping seats with your fellow graduands. Guests will be seated on a first come, first served basis. All attendees are requested to remain seated after entering the Theatre. Please be punctual as the ceremony will commence on time and latecomers will not be allowed into come, first served basis. All attendees are requested to remain seated after entering the Theatre. Please be punctual as the ceremony will commence on time and latecomers will not be allowed into the venue. Children below the age of seven will not be permitted to enter the Theatre for the ceremony. Etiquette & Dress Code The Convocation ceremony is a joyous and memorable event for graduands and their loved ones. To preserve the decorum of the ceremony, all graduands and guests are to refrain from disrespectful or inappropriate behaviour that may disrupt the event’s solemnity. We seek your kind understanding to focus on celebrating the achievements of our graduands. Guests are requested to dress appropriately and avoid casual or inappropriate attire, such as slippers or flip-flops. Graduands are advised not to bring large bags or backpacks, as there may not be a designated area for storing personal belongings. SIT is not liable for lost, stolen or damaged personal belongings. Graduands are advised not to bring large bags or backpacks, as there may not be a designated area for storing personal belongings. SIT is not liable for lost, stolen or damaged personal belongings. Graduands are advised to comply with the dress code and dress appropriately for the occasion. Men Ladies Collared long-sleeved shirt and dark-coloured trousers. Shirt/blouse and dark-coloured trousers or skirt (knee-length), or a dress (knee-length). Covered shoes with socks. Shoes of a comfortable height. Traditional attire (e.g. sari) may be worn. Graduands who are inappropriately dressed (i.e. shorts, jeans, casual pants, slippers or track shoes), or wearing an academic dress with the wrong hood colour or design, will not be presented on stage. Presentation of Graduands Graduands will be prompted to line up for the presentation on stage. Show your Graduand Admission Card to the marshals and line up quickly. When lining up, ensure you keep the same sequence. When you walk up the stage, wait at the up for the presentation on stage. Show your Graduand Admission Card to the marshals and line up quickly. When lining up, ensure you keep the same sequence. When you walk up the stage, wait at the waiting line for your name to be read. When your name is read, walk briskly towards the presenter to receive the certificate folder, and pose for your photo. After this, walk towards the other end to exit the stage. Ushers will direct you to the certificate collection counter before you return to your seat. Please refrain from exiting the Theatre or going to the guests’ seats. End of Ceremony Light refreshments will be served. Graduation bears and plaques are available for purchase at the reception area. Photos You may access the photos of the ceremony from 23 October 2024 via the official photographer’s website. title: 0641_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_convocation-2024_information-graduands.html
text: Home About SIT Convocation 2024 Information for Graduands Information for Graduands Important Dates Key Activities Period Registration for Convocation 2024 12 to 23 August 2024 Ordering of Academic Dress 12 to 23 August 2024 Collection of Academic Dress 24 September to 15 October 2024 Convocation 2024 Ceremonies 16 to 18 October 2024 Returning of Academic Dress 23 October to 3 November 2024 Collection of Degree Parchment (For graduates who did not attend the ceremony) From 28 October 2024 chevron--up Convocation Registration Checklist Registration period for Convocation 2024: 12 to 23 August 2024 Eligible graduands will receive an email invitation (via their SIT email) to register for Convocation 2024 in early August. Step 1: Check your Eligibility To be eligible for Convocation 2024, you have to: Fulfill all graduation requirements * Be conferred with a degree in Trimester 1, 2 or 3 of AY2023/24 * If you fail to fulfil the graduation requirements, your attendance registration and you have to: Fulfill all graduation requirements * Be conferred with a degree in Trimester 1, 2 or 3 of AY2023/24 * If you fail to fulfil the graduation requirements, your attendance registration and academic dress order will be voided. Step 2: Register your Attendance via IN4SIT Log in to IN4SIT Select Graduation Select Ceremony Registration During the registration, you are reminded to: Verify your contact details in IN4SIT. Please ensure that your information is accurate and up-to-date. Indicate your attendance and number of guests ( up to two ) whom you are inviting to attend the ceremony. Indicate in the ‘Remarks’ section if you, or your guests, are arriving in a wheelchair, require special assistance, or have mobility challenges. Ensure your registration is properly submitted by clicking the ‘Submit’ button on the final page. Step 3: Order and Collect your Academic Dress Upon registration of your attendance for the ceremony, order your academic dress with the university’s by clicking the ‘Submit’ button on the final page. Step 3: Order and Collect your Academic Dress Upon registration of your attendance for the ceremony, order your academic dress with the university’s official Academic Dress vendor – Serangoon Broadway. Please see “ Academic Dress ” for more information. Step 4: Settle Outstanding Fees (if any) Please settle any outstanding fees and loans before October 2024 to avoid having your degree parchment withheld. For enquiries regarding Convocation 2024, please contact the Registrar’s Office . chevron--up Collection of Admission Card and Academic Dress Graduand Admission Card You will receive a Graduand Admission Card after you have successfully registered for the Convocation Ceremony. This card includes details such as your name, student ID, and allocated graduand seat number. You are required to retain the card throughout the ceremony. Please collect it along with your academic dress from our official academic dress vendor, Serangoon and allocated graduand seat number. You are required to retain the card throughout the ceremony. Please collect it along with your academic dress from our official academic dress vendor, Serangoon Broadway. Academic Dress You are required to order your academic dress from our official vendor, Serangoon Broadway, from 12 to 23 August 2024 . More details will be provided when you register your attendance for the ceremony via IN4SIT. Please note that appointments for the collection of the academic dress are on a first-come, first-served basis. You may refer to this page for the types of academic dress. Collection Venue The collection venue for your Graduand Admission Card and academic dress will be as follows. You may collect these items from 24 September to 15 October 2024 . Serangoon Broadway 1365 Serangoon Road Singapore 328244 Phone: +65 6293 3998 Email: graduations@serangoonbroadway.com Business Hours: Tuesday – Sunday / 10am – 8pm (Closed on Mondays and all public holidays) During 1365 Serangoon Road Singapore 328244 Phone: +65 6293 3998 Email: graduations@serangoonbroadway.com Business Hours: Tuesday – Sunday / 10am – 8pm (Closed on Mondays and all public holidays) During the collection of your academic dress, you will be required to make payment for the following: Security Deposit (refundable) : S$150.00 Rental Fee for Academic Dress : S$40.00 Returning of Academic Dress After the ceremony, you are reminded to return the academic dress to Serangoon Broadway from 23 October to 3 November 2024 . The deposit will be refunded when you return the academic dress during this period in good condition * . Should you return the academic dress after this period, or if it is not found to be in good condition * , the security deposit will be forfeited. * An academic dress is deemed to be in "good condition" if there are no cuttings/alterations of any kinds, burns, tears, rips in fabric, holes on velvet or satin, discolourations, irremovable stains, scotch tape marks, is deemed to be in "good condition" if there are no cuttings/alterations of any kinds, burns, tears, rips in fabric, holes on velvet or satin, discolourations, irremovable stains, scotch tape marks, damaged or missing buttons/tassel or dented mortar boards. Degree Parchment & Transcripts For students conferred in Trimesters 1 and 2, you will be notified via your SIT email when the documents are ready for collection. Uncollected degree parchments and official transcripts will be available for collection at the Convocation ceremonies. For students conferred in Trimester 3, the degree parchments and official transcripts will be available for collection at the Convocation ceremonies. You are strongly advised to settle all outstanding fees ahead of the ceremony. Graduands with outstanding fees will have their degree parchments withheld. You may refer to the Student Intranet for more information on degree parchment, transcripts, and other information relating to Convocation 2024. OpenCerts will have their degree parchments withheld. You may refer to the Student Intranet for more information on degree parchment, transcripts, and other information relating to Convocation 2024. OpenCerts Under the OpenCerts initiative led by SkillsFuture Singapore and the Government Technology Agency, graduands will also be issued a digital OpenCerts parchment. The OpenCerts parchment will automatically be added to your Skills Passport in your MySkillsFuture account. You can view or download your OpenCerts parchment by logging in at the MySkillsFuture website. With your OpenCerts certificate, potential employers or learning institutes may verify your academic qualification obtained from SIT. Graduands will be notified via the SIT lifelong email account when the OpenCerts parchment is available. For more information on OpenCerts, please visit https://opencerts.io/ . chevron--up Guest e-Tickets Registered graduands may register for e-tickets for up to two guests. Each e-ticket admits one For more information on OpenCerts, please visit https://opencerts.io/ . chevron--up Guest e-Tickets Registered graduands may register for e-tickets for up to two guests. Each e-ticket admits one person. Graduands will receive the e-tickets via your SIT email address by end September 2024. You are advised to forward the email and ticket details to your guests, as there are separate entry points for graduands and guests. If your guests are entering together: The email may be forwarded to them. Both e-tickets will be scanned together. If your guests are entering separately: Download the e-tickets from the email. Save them as two separate PDFs. The separate PDFs can be emailed to your respective guests, or you may print them and pass the printed tickets to them separately. Either a printed copy or soft copy of the tickets must be presented for scanning at the point of entry to enter the Theatre. Children below the age of seven will not be permitted to enter the Theatre for the ceremony. or soft copy of the tickets must be presented for scanning at the point of entry to enter the Theatre. title: 0646_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_convocation-2025.html
text: Home Convocation 2025 Congratulations, SIT Class of 2025! Graduation season marks both an ending and a beginning. It bookends your academic journey at SIT and signals the exciting start of your next chapter as industry-ready professionals. This year’s Convocation is especially meaningful as it is the first to be held at our new Punggol Campus. More than a venue, it symbolises SIT’s bold future as part of a vibrant innovation ecosystem where learning, industry and academia converge. As Singapore’s University for Industry, we’ve challenged you through applied learning, real-world projects, and deep industry immersion. You now graduate with not only deep skills, but also the resilience and lifelong learning mindset that define our SITizens. You are ready to thrive in a fast-changing world and across a lifetime of careers. Even as you move forward, we invite you to stay connected. Return to home ground to upskill and reskill, and to inspire your juniors by sharing your experiences and and across a lifetime of careers. Even as you move forward, we invite you to stay connected. Return to home ground to upskill and reskill, and to inspire your juniors by sharing your experiences and mentoring them. Congratulations once again! We’re proud to celebrate this milestone with you and your loved ones. title: 0650_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_convocation-2025_frequently-asked-questions.html
text: Home Convocation 2025 Frequently Asked Questions Frequently Asked Questions For Graduands chevron--up How do I know if I am eligible to attend Convocation 2025? You are eligible to attend Convocation 2025 if you have been conferred with a degree in AY2024/25 Trimester 1, 2 and 3. Additionally, you should receive an invitation email in your SIT email to register for Convocation 2025 by end August 2025. chevron--up I have registered for Convocation 2025 via IN4SIT. What are the next steps? You should receive a confirmation email in your SIT email notifying you that your registration is successful. Next, please ensure that you complete all items in the Convocation Preparation Checklist before your ceremony day. chevron--up What should I do if I did not receive a confirmation email after registering for Convocation 2025 in IN4SIT? A confirmation email will be sent to your SIT email if the registration is successful. If you did not receive a confirmation email, please ensure that you have for Convocation 2025 in IN4SIT? A confirmation email will be sent to your SIT email if the registration is successful. If you did not receive a confirmation email, please ensure that you have clicked on the ‘Submit’ button at the end of the registration process. Otherwise, please resubmit your registration. If the problem persists, you may write to Convocation@SingaporeTech.edu.sg for assistance. chevron--up What should I do if I do not have access to my free-for-life SIT email? You may log into the Self-Service Portal to retrieve/reset your password or write in to IThelpdesk@Singaporetech.edu.sg . chevron--up When will I receive the e-tickets for guests? You will receive the e-tickets for guests via your SIT email at the end of September 2025. chevron--up Can I request for more than 2 guest e-tickets? Graduands are entitled to a maximum of 2 guest e-tickets only. If you have more than 2 guests, they are welcome to view the livestream from Lectorial 7, E2 Level 1. Entry to Lectorial 7 e-tickets? Graduands are entitled to a maximum of 2 guest e-tickets only. If you have more than 2 guests, they are welcome to view the livestream from Lectorial 7, E2 Level 1. Entry to Lectorial 7 is first come, first served. chevron--up What do I need to bring on the ceremony day? The following are mandatory items to be brought to your ceremony: Graduand admission card; Academic dress, hood and cap; and Graduands and guests are advised not to bring large bags. SIT is not liable for any lost, stolen or damaged personal belongings. chevron--up Where and when can I collect my academic dress? Please find information on the collection of academic dress here . chevron--up What is the dress code for the ceremony? Please find the dress code for graduands here . chevron--up Where and when should I put on my academic dress on ceremony day? You may robe your academic dress at the Auditorium foyer at least 45 minutes before the start of the ceremony. chevron--up What will I receive during the I put on my academic dress on ceremony day? You may robe your academic dress at the Auditorium foyer at least 45 minutes before the start of the ceremony. chevron--up What will I receive during the ceremony? If you did not collectyour degree parchment and transcript prior to the ceremony, you will receive the following items on ceremony day: Degree parchment* Official academic transcript Record of Transferable Skills (RTS), for applicable degree programmes only Award(s), if any If you had collected your degree parchment and transcript before Convocation, you will receive the following: Award(s), if any Congratulatory letter *Degree parchments will be withheld if there are any outstanding fees as of the day of your ceremony. Please contact Student Finance via StudentFinance@SingaporeTech.edu.sg for enquiries on outstanding fees. chevron--up Am I an award recipient? Please refer to the list of award recipients for 2025 here . chevron--up What is the sequence of graduands being presented for enquiries on outstanding fees. chevron--up Am I an award recipient? Please refer to the list of award recipients for 2025 here . chevron--up What is the sequence of graduands being presented on stage? The graduand listing for Convocation 2025 will be published here on 13 October 2025. You may refer to the list for the sequence of graduands bring presented on stage. chevron--up (applicable to Trimester 1 & 2 graduands) Will I be presented on stage if I have already collected my degree parchment and transcript during the early collection period? You will still be presented on stage during the ceremony if you had collected your degree parchment and transcript during the early collection period. chevron--up Will there be official photos taken during my stage presentation? Photographs will be taken during your stage presentation. You may access and purchase the photos here from 24 October 2025 . chevron--up Are there allocated wheelchair spaces in the Auditorium? Yes. Additionally, be taken during your stage presentation. You may access and purchase the photos here from 24 October 2025 . chevron--up Are there allocated wheelchair spaces in the Auditorium? Yes. Additionally, graduands and guests with mobility challenges may take the lift located at E3 Level 1, assisted by our ushers. If you require special assistance on stage, please write to Convocation@Singaporetech.edu.sg by 1 October 2025 . chevron--up What is the duration of the ceremony? The ceremony is estimated to be approximately 1 hour 30 minutes in duration. The actual duration may vary for each session. chevron--up Will there be refreshments provided? Snack boxes will be distributed after the ceremony to graduands and guests who have attended the ceremony. Please note that eating and drinking in the Auditorium is prohibited. Additionally, there will be food stalls available at E6. Graduands may redeem a food item using the voucher that will be distributed on ceremony day. chevron--up I am not able to is prohibited. Additionally, there will be food stalls available at E6. Graduands may redeem a food item using the voucher that will be distributed on ceremony day. chevron--up I am not able to attend SIT Convocation 2025. Can a representative collect my degree parchment and transcript on my behalf? It is not possible to send a representative to collect your degree parchment and transcript on your behalf during the period of Convocation 2025. If you are unable to attend SIT Convocation 2025 and have yet to collect your degree parchment and transcript, you may do so from 27 October 2025 . Please write in to Registrar@SingaporeTech.edu.sg to arrange collection after the ceremony. For Guests chevron--up What do I need to bring for the ceremony? Please bring your e-ticket in softcopy or hardcopy and ensure that each guest has 1 e-ticket . E-tickets will be scanned before guests enter the Auditorium. If you did not receive your e-ticket, please check with the graduand. E-tickets will be and ensure that each guest has 1 e-ticket . E-tickets will be scanned before guests enter the Auditorium. If you did not receive your e-ticket, please check with the graduand. E-tickets will be sent to all graduands’ SIT email by the end of September 2025. Guests without e-tickets or under the age of 7 will not be permitted entry to the Auditorium. They may watch the livestream from Lectorial 7, E2 Level 1. Entry to Lectorial 7 is first come, first served. Graduands and guests are advised not to bring large bags. SIT is not liable for lost, stolen or damaged personal belongings. chevron--up What is the dress code? Please refer to the dress code here . chevron--up Is the event open to the public? The ceremony held at the Auditorium is a closed event and is only accessible to graduands and guests with valid e-tickets. However, SIT Punggol Campus is open to the public. chevron--up Can I bring flowers to the ceremony? You may bring flowers. However, all flowers must be deposited at the guests with valid e-tickets. However, SIT Punggol Campus is open to the public. chevron--up Can I bring flowers to the ceremony? You may bring flowers. However, all flowers must be deposited at the flower deposit counter located at E3, Level 1M before the ceremony as flowers in the Auditorium is prohibited. You may collect your flowers from the counter after the ceremony. chevron--up Will there be flowers and merchandise sold on campus? Flowers and merchandise will be available for purchase at E6 Level 1. chevron--up Are there allocated wheelchair spaces in the Auditorium? Yes. Additionally, graduands and guests requiring mobility assistance may take the lift from E3 Level 1, assisted by our ushers. chevron--up Will there be refreshments provided? Snack boxes will be distributed after the ceremony to graduands and guests who have attended the ceremony. Please note that eating and drinking in the auditorium is prohibited. Additionally, there will be food for purchase at E6. For other ceremony to graduands and guests who have attended the ceremony. Please note that eating and drinking in the auditorium is prohibited. Additionally, there will be food for purchase at E6. For other enquiries, please write to Convocation@SingaporeTech.edu.sg . text: Home Competency-based Stackable Programmes Competency‑based Stackable Programmes Competency-based Stackable Micro-credential (CSM) degrees are your key to adapting to the evolving demands of the job market and growing your career. Designed for adult learners, our CSM degree programmes equip you with a strong technical foundation and real-world experience to meet industry standards. They are broken down into micro-credentials, allowing you to master skills that matter most to you, when you want to. Each micro-credential earns you a specialist certificate which you can stack towards a full degree. You can also fast-track your studies, as SIT’s CSM degree pathway offers credit or exemptions for the knowledge and skills you've already mastered. Experience the flexibility of pacing your own learning through our blended approach of online and face-to-face learning. You'll also be supported on this journey by our dedicated success coaches who are here to help you navigate your unique own learning through our blended approach of online and face-to-face learning. You'll also be supported on this journey by our dedicated success coaches who are here to help you navigate your unique learning path. If you're focused on just-in-time upskilling, you can explore our individual micro-credentials . title: 0658_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_customised-free-standing-automated-external-defibrillator-stand.html
text: Home Customised Free-Standing Automated External Defibrillator Stand Customised Free-Standing Automated External Defibrillator Stand Automated External Defibrillator (AED) boxes are typically mounted on building walls . Placing them at visible and easy-to-access locations can help save the lives of cardiac-arrest victims during emergencies. However, they are currently not available at large outdoor areas and parks where there are no walls to mount the boxes on. Our Community Partner Singapore Civil Defence Force (SCDF) Seng Kang Fire Station Our Faculty Members Associate Professor Alfred Tan, Engineering Cluster Challenges To design a free-standing solar-powered stand to mount the AED. To protect the AED (between $2,500 and $3,000 each) from tampering or theft via a surveillance system that can alert SCDF on the intrusion. Key Innovation Using technology and simple innovation to enable an AED to be sited, secure, and yet accessible (such as,weather-proof housing for outdoor use) at that can alert SCDF on the intrusion. Key Innovation Using technology and simple innovation to enable an AED to be sited, secure, and yet accessible (such as,weather-proof housing for outdoor use) at remote areas for use during real emergencies. The free-standing solar-powered AED stand is equipped with a CCTV camera alarm and wireless 3G communications to send instant messages and images to SCDF. Upon usage of the AED, photographs of the user with date and time stamp will be captured and sent to SCDF’s and SPF’s Operations Centre via SMS. SCDF will also be able to dispatch an ambulance immediately when the AED is engaged. The CCTV surveillance is primarily to deter malicious acts, theft, and mischief.

text: Home CXO Lean Summit 2018 CXO Lean Summit 2018 Placing Patients at the heart of Healthcare Transformation. John Shook, Executive Chairman of the Lean Global Network and Lean Enterprise Institute, USA, delivered his keynote speech on Lean Thinking in Healthcare. He shared how organisations can build the capability of their employees and align their problem-solving activities to strategic organisational goals. Denise Bennett, Executive Director of the Australasian Lean Healthcare Network and Lean Coach of the Lean Global Network also delivered her plenary talk on ‘Placing the patient front, and centre, using Lean thinking and practice’. This was followed by company sharings by National Heart Centre Singapore, Yishun Community Hospital and Mount Alvernia Hospital, alongside SIT Faculty, A/Prof using Lean thinking and practice’. This was followed by company sharings by National Heart Centre Singapore, Yishun Community Hospital and Mount Alvernia Hospital, alongside SIT Faculty, A/Prof Genedine Lim and A/Prof Malcolm Low, on their experiences in Lean transformation. More than 300 participants from many healthcare organisations at different stages of their Lean transformation journey brought the summit to a resounding success. download Download Presentation Slides Click here to access John Shook's presentation slides chevron--up Featured Speakers Keynote Speech John Shook Executive Chairman Lean Global Network and Lean Enterprise Institute, USA What is True North for an organisation? How do we develop effective problem-solvers? In this annual Lean conference, John will share how leaders can align the problem-solving activities of employees with strategic priorities to radically and sustainably improve organisational performance. John will also share the benefits of the Lean leaders can align the problem-solving activities of employees with strategic priorities to radically and sustainably improve organisational performance. John will also share the benefits of the Lean Transformation Framework and Strategy Deployment, and how it is applied in the context of healthcare organisations. In the area of healthcare, patients are THE most important customer. With an increasing focus on patient-centric care which goes beyond quality healthcare services, Denise will share how organisations can transform by designing their processes and practices around patients, thus delivering true value to them. Hear from A/Profs Genedine and Malcolm as they share about how SIT collaborates with local healthcare organisations and companies who are embarking on their own Lean journeys. chevron--up Programme Overview 21 & 23 April 2018: Pre-Summit Masterclasses Topic Lean Coach Lean Masterclass: Enhance organisational excellence using the Lean Transformation Framework and Value Stream Improvement Karl Ohaus and A/Prof Mustafa Shabbir Kurbanhusen Lean Masterclass: Beyond Lean Tools and Projects for Healthcare Denise Bennett 24 April 2018: CXO Lean Summit 2018 Time programme 8.15am Registration and Breakfast 9.00am Welcome Address Prof Tan Thiam Soon, President, Singapore Institute of Technology 9.10 am Denise Bennett 24 April 2018: CXO Lean Summit 2018 Time programme 8.15am Registration and Breakfast 9.00am Welcome Address Prof Tan Thiam Soon, President, Singapore Institute of Technology 9.10 am Opening Address A/Prof Benjamin Ong, Director of Medical Services, Ministry of Health 9.20 am Keynote Speech Mr John Shook, Executive Chairman, Lean Global Network and Lean Enterprise Institute, USA 10.00 am Plenary Session Ms Denise Bennett, Executive Director, Australasian Lean Healthcare Network and Lean Coach, Lean Global Network 10.30 am Tea Break 11.00 am Sharing by Local Healthcare Organisations and SIT Faculty 11.45 am Panel Discussion with Guest Speakers 12.30 pm Closing Remarks & Lucky Draw 12.45 pm Lunch Reception 2.00 pm End of Programme chevron--up Pre-Summit Masterclass I: Enhance organisational excellence using the Lean Transformation Framework and Value Stream Improvement Course Synopsis A value stream is the process steps that occur in order to provide the product, service organisational excellence using the Lean Transformation Framework and Value Stream Improvement Course Synopsis A value stream is the process steps that occur in order to provide the product, service and/or experience for a customer. All organisations have one or more value streams that deliver value to their customers. In this 2-day masterclass, explore how the Lean Transformation Framework (LTF) provides a lens for organisations to embark on a Lean Transformation for productivity enhancement through the powerful methodology of Value Stream Improvement. What You Will Learn Participants will learn about the 5 key elements of the Lean Transformation Framework: Strategy deployment Process improvement Leader behaviour & management systems Manpower (capability) development Organisational mindset & culture Through highly engaging presentations, group activities and hands-on exercises, participants will learn: What is the Lean Transformation Framework? Why the situational approach in the LTF & culture Through highly engaging presentations, group activities and hands-on exercises, participants will learn: What is the Lean Transformation Framework? Why the situational approach in the LTF is critical to a successful Lean Transformation Use the Value Stream Approach to begin Strategy Deployment Chart Primary and Support Value Streams How to concurrently improve the capability and capacity of both value streams and people The cycle of ‘Plan, Do, Check, Act’ Problem-solving as a powerful continuous improvement tool How to engage your organisation in a Continuous Improvement mindset Course Outline Introduction to the Lean Transformation Framework Value stream improvement methods Creating a sustainable Lean culture Building people capability for problem solving and continuous improvement Sharing of case studies chevron--up Pre-Summit Masterclass II: Beyond Lean Tools and Projects for Healthcare Synopsis Many healthcare organisations introduce Lean thinking concepts and tools in Sharing of case studies chevron--up Pre-Summit Masterclass II: Beyond Lean Tools and Projects for Healthcare Synopsis Many healthcare organisations introduce Lean thinking concepts and tools in the form of projects. These projects feel like yet another thing to learn about and include in our already busy work day. This masterclass will explore the use of Lean Thinking and Practice as a management or operating system, providing a framework for creating stability in work areas, deploying and meeting important team level metrics that link to organisational goals, and addressing problems that occur on a daily basis. Daily management systems ensure that the value stream improvement sustains beyond the life of the project and ensure that precious project resources were not wasted. title: 0664_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_designing-activity-space-dementia-patients.html
text: Home Designing Activity Space for Dementia Patients Designing Activity Space for Dementia Patients Dementia is the loss of intellectual abilities (such as thinking, remembering, and reasoning), which interferes with a person’s daily function. It is not a disease, but rather a group of symptoms that may accompany certain diseases. As a result, the mental abilities of the person with dementia decline. This leads to failing memory, deterioration of intellectual function, and personality changes. There is no definitive cure for dementia as the exact causes are unclear. However, it is possible to minimise some of the common symptoms with medication, in addition to patient-centred care. Therapies, such as reality orientation and reminiscence, also help to manage some of these symptoms. The occupational therapists carry out activities of daily living training for seniors to encourage independence within the community, as well as in nursing homes for residing patients. Our Community Partner therapists carry out activities of daily living training for seniors to encourage independence within the community, as well as in nursing homes for residing patients. Our Community Partner Kwong Wai Shiu Hospital Our Faculty Members Assistant Professor Agnes Xue, Business, Communication and Design Cluster Associate Professor May Lim, Health and Social Sciences Cluster Glasgow School of Arts Faculty Member Mr Lee Seung-Ho Challenges Achieving person-centred care for dementia patients Designing appropriate activity/therapy spaces that can stimulate the senses, while considering user experiences, nostalgia, sustainability and fittings, as well as accessibility and patient safety Key Innovation Various age-friendly features within different themes to make the activity spaces assuring, welcoming, and conducive to optimise therapeutic effects for the users.

title: 0665_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_designing-recycling-receptacle-promote-green-living.html
text: Home Designing a recycling receptacle to promote green living Designing a recycling receptacle to promote green living As part of their efforts to promote correct recycling practices and encourage higher recycling rates among Singaporeans, the National Environment Agency (NEA) invited SIT students to contribute proposed designs for a recycling receptacle for households in Singapore. This project is a collaboration with the Public Waste Collectors (PWCs). In addition to being compact, foldable, and attractive, the proposed receptacle must be sufficiently spacious to accommodate bottles, egg cartons, and small boxes. The receptacle must also be expandable, sturdy, waterproof, and washable. Should a design by SIT students be accepted, it will be shared with the Public Waste Collectors (PWCs) for mass production and distribution to all households in Singapore. This project is timely as it serves our national sustainability initiative and coincides with SIT’s shift to our new Punggol for mass production and distribution to all households in Singapore. This project is timely as it serves our national sustainability initiative and coincides with SIT’s shift to our new Punggol campus. Do look out for the recycling receptacle created by our students at the Punggol Digital District.

title: 0666_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_door-breaching-equipment-frontline-firefighting-and-rescue-work.html
text: Home Door Breaching Equipment for Frontline Firefighting and Rescue Work Door Breaching Equipment for Frontline Firefighting and Rescue Work SCDF firefighters typically carry, amongst other tools and equipment, a conventional halligan tool and a battering ram for their firefighting and rescue operations. They can weigh as much as 29kg in all and the heavy load can degrade operational efficiency, particularly when the firefighters need to mount multiple staircases with the heavy equipment, in order to reach the rescue site. Our Community Partner Singapore Civil Defence Force (SCDF) Ang Mo Kio Fire Station Our Faculty Members Associate Professor Patrick Chua, Engineering Cluster Challenges To innovate on existing door breaching equipment to make them more compact and lighter in weight To improve and ease a firefighter’s entry into a building during operations Key Innovation A halligan tool, modified with a chisel and integrated with an attached hammer, can be extended up to 80cm for improve and ease a firefighter’s entry into a building during operations Key Innovation A halligan tool, modified with a chisel and integrated with an attached hammer, can be extended up to 80cm for maximum prying leverage, particularly in tight spaces. A hollow battering ram, made of stainless steel or PE-UHMW material, which has a detachable cap, allows the battering ram’s interior to be filled up with water quickly to increase its overall mass by 5kg. The total combined weight for both items is 18kg, which is 63% of the weight of conventional halligan tool and battering ram, currently used by SCDF.

title: 0667_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_encouraging-healthy-eating-habits-children-and-parents-through-mobile-app-gamification.html
text: Home Encouraging healthy eating habits in children and parents through mobile app gamification Encouraging healthy eating habits in children and parents through mobile app gamification Many studies show that children who learn about healthy eating habits early on in life tend to maintain these habits in adulthood. Inspired by the NTUC Bright Horizon Fund (BHF), which uses targeted interventions to support children from low-income families, a group of SIT faculty members came together to raise awareness about the nutritional values of food and inspire healthier eating habits. The SIT-BHF collaboration enabled an exchange of ideas on how to create meaningful behavioural changes in eating and food purchasing habits for children and parents. The collaborators proposed adding game mechanics in the BHF mobile app to encourage pre-schoolers and their caregivers to learn about nutritious food, cost-effective healthy diets, and how to develop good eating habits. The team improved app in the BHF mobile app to encourage pre-schoolers and their caregivers to learn about nutritious food, cost-effective healthy diets, and how to develop good eating habits. The team improved app interactions through a game format. Through the app, children could access information on nutrition that would influence healthy eating behaviours. The app also provided reminders to parents on how to make healthier food choices for their families. The project team that spearheaded the project comprised Mr Oran Zane Devilly; Associate Professor (A/P) Tan Chek Tien; and Mr Dan Chia from the Infocomm Technology cluster as well as A/P Wang Mei Yin from the Health and Social Sciences cluster, and Assistant Professor Verena Tan from the Food, Chemical and Biotechnology cluster. Students from the ICT4004 Design Thinking class in the Infocomm Technology cluster are working on the gamification design in collaboration with students from other disciplines. The main project team will also benefit from the Design Thinking class in the Infocomm Technology cluster are working on the gamification design in collaboration with students from other disciplines. The main project team will also benefit from the knowledge-sharing on food safety by students from the Food Technology programme (Chemical Engineering and Food Technology). The students will ensure that information related to food storage as well as the safe handling of raw food ingredients and cooked food is relevant and accurate. Students from the Dietetics and Nutrition programme (Health and Social Sciences) will also support the project. They will provide information on affordable and nutritious foods, appropriate eating habits, and fun facts on food. Once completed, the mobile game is expected to make a positive social impact on users’ eating habits.

title: 0668_www.singaporetech.edu.sg_energy-efficiency-technology-centre.html
text: Home Research Energy Efficiency Technology Centre Energy Efficiency Technology Centre In collaboration with the National Environment Agency (NEA), the Energy Efficiency Technology Centre (EETC) aims to be the leading technology innovation centre that supports local Small & Medium Enterprises (SMEs) in energy efficiency initiatives. EETC aspires to promote the development of energy efficiency capability and the adoption of new technologies in the local energy ecosystem for the industrial sectors. Value to Industry EETC supports local industries in energy efficiency initiatives and helps to address both short and long-term needs in the local EE ecosystem through the following: Catalyse EE improvements at SMEs through the provision of energy consultancy services. Develop a pipeline of engineering undergraduates in industrial energy efficiency. Upskill existing engineers or EE practitioners through practical hands-on training courses. What We Do Catalyse Energy Efficiency Initiatives We undergraduates in industrial energy efficiency. Upskill existing engineers or EE practitioners through practical hands-on training courses. What We Do Catalyse Energy Efficiency Initiatives We help SMEs be more competitive by reducing energy costs through low-cost high- quality energy assessments.
